entries.title: Recipe: Javauneeka Jacobs’ glazed Mexican white sweet potatoes with chipotle crema
entries.summary: <p>If you haven’t had Mexican white sweet potatoes — they’re available at almost every Mexican market and at many well-stocked groceries — you’re missing one of my favorite things on our planet. The flavor is lighter, more gently aromatic, more lilting than orange sweet potatoes, which is why I return to them over and over.</p><p>This recipe involves several steps, including beautifully cutting the sweet potatoes “hasselback style.” Think of this as a show-stopping side dish for a special dinner. Folks will talk about it for months to come.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c20000" name="image-c20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3786a66/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4284x2404+0+1654/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2Fa0%2Ffe02053b425eb4a39b97a9b43a1d%2Fimg-8464.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aad3695/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4284x2404+0+1654/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2Fa0%2Ffe02053b425eb4a39b97a9b43a1d%2Fimg-8464.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/44ea387/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4284x2404+0+1654/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2Fa0%2Ffe02053b425eb4a39b97a9b43a1d%2Fimg-8464.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Javauneeka Jacobs and Rick Bayless with camotes bravos." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/44ea387/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4284x2404+0+1654/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2Fa0%2Ffe02053b425eb4a39b97a9b43a1d%2Fimg-8464.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dc97e6c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4284x2404+0+1654/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2Fa0%2Ffe02053b425eb4a39b97a9b43a1d%2Fimg-8464.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/44ea387/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4284x2404+0+1654/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd3%2Fa0%2Ffe02053b425eb4a39b97a9b43a1d%2Fimg-8464.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Javauneeka Jacobs and Rick Bayless hold up a platter of camote bravo, a dish created by Jacobs.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Camote Bravo: Glazed Mexican white sweet potatoes with chipotle crema</h3><p>Serves 6 to 8</p><p><b>Ingredients</b><br>4 medium (about 14 ounces each) Mexican white sweet potatoes (camotes blancos)<br>About 1 ¾ cup vegetable or olive oil, plus a little for sauteing the onions (divided use)<br>2 egg yolks plus 1 whole egg<br>2 tablespoons lime juice<br>5 canned chipotle chiles en adobo (divided use)<br>¼ cup Mexican crema, creme fraiche or sour cream<br>¼ cup Mexican queso añejo or other garnishing cheese like Parmesan or Romano, plus more for garnishing<br>A small handful of chopped cilantro<br>About ¼ cup agave syrup or honey<br>1 bunch (about 5) knob onions (1-inch knobs are perfect), cut crosswise into ¼-inch slices</p><p><b>Directions</b><br>Cut and bake the sweet potatoes. Heat the oven to 400°F. </p><p>One by one, cut the sweet potatoes hasselback-style: Lay a wooden spoon close along the length of the potato, then use a sharp knife to cut down through the potato in ⅛-inch intervals, stopping when you reach the spoon. (This ensures you don’t cut completely through the potato.) </p><p>Lay the potatoes in a baking dish and heavily brush with oil, making sure some of the oil gets down between the slices. Sprinkle generously with salt — again, making sure some gets down between the slices. Cover with foil and bake for 45 minutes. Remove the foil and bake 10 to 15 more minutes, until tender and slightly crispy. </p><p>While the potatoes are baking, prepare the crema, glaze and knob onions.</p><p><b>Make the chipotle crema.</b> In a food processor or blender, combine the egg yolks, whole egg and lime juice. Turn on to medium speed and, when thoroughly blended, slowly drizzle in 1 1/2 cups of oil to create a thick mayonnaise.</p><p>Add 2 of the chipotle chiles, the crema, añejo cheese and cilantro. Process until smooth, then taste and season with salt, usually a generous ½ teaspoon.</p><p><b>Make the glaze.</b> Rinse out the food processor, then add to the 3 remaining chipotle chiles, the agave syrup or honey and a pinch salt. Blend until smooth.</p><p><b>Cook the knob onions.</b> Lightly skim a large (10-inch) skillet with oil and set over medium.</p><p>Scoop in the knob onions and cook, stirring regularly, until soft, 4 to 5 minutes.</p><p><b>Serve.</b> Once the potatoes are tender, brush them generously with the glaze and put back into the oven for 5 minutes. Spread the crema liberally on a serving platter (you’ll likely have about ½ cup extra to use on sandwiches or in dressings), then use a spatula to transfer the sweet potatoes to the platter, nestling them into the crema. Scatter the knob onions over the top, then sprinkle everything with more añejo cheese.</p>
entries.author: Javauneeka Jacobs

entries.title: Ámmo'naka' explores connections between humans and land
entries.summary: <p>When “Ámmo'naka” receives its world premiere on Earth Day on Wednesday, a giant stacked choral chord will burst out of silence, with a wide-open, ringing-crystal voicing, sung fortissimo. The sopranos will squeal out some of the highest sung notes composers ever call for. The chord will suddenly stop, and the choir all speak, each in their own rhythm, the first line of the text, a babel of unintelligibility because of the overlap.</p><p>Audiences will hear another sudden silence, and the choir will sing the words, together and now understandable when “Ámmo'naka” premieres at Loyola University Chicago through the efforts of the EcoVoice Project.</p><p>The words are “Ámmo'naka poshki',” meaning “In a beginning / Our Mother” in the Chickasaw language. The libretto, original poetry written in Chickasaw by the poet Lokosh, tells the traditional Chickasaw story about the creation of the world and the relationship between humans and the land.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-380000" name="module-380000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><a class="Link" href="https://luc.universitytickets.com/w/event.aspx?id=2209" target="_blank" ><b>Ámmo’naka</b></a></h3><br><b>Where:</b> Mundelein Center for the Fine and Performing Arts, Loyola University, 1020 W. Sheridan Rd.<br><b>When:</b> April 22, 7:30 p.m.<br><b>Info: </b>Tickets are $5–$15 <br></div> </div><p>“What I tried to do with this project is convey our traditional worldview that the earth is considered our mother,” said Lokosh, a maker and poet based in Oklahoma. In the first movement, the creator called He Who Sits Above selects the crayfish to bring mud up from the depths to fashion the land, and the mountains and valleys are shaped by the wings of a bird, traditionally a raven or a buzzard. “I prefer the buzzard because I think it’s an unlikely progenitor,” Lokosh said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-portrait-right> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-060000" name="image-060000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium-3x4"> <source type="image/webp" width="375" height="500" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cf7b72a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x1365+0+85/resize/375x500!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F04%2F596bfbea4b65b90cf8621f5ca646%2Flokosh-photo.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSI1MDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjM3NXB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="375" height="500" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ed11173/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x1365+0+85/resize/375x500!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F04%2F596bfbea4b65b90cf8621f5ca646%2Flokosh-photo.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSI1MDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjM3NXB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Lokosh photo.png" width="375" height="500" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ed11173/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x1365+0+85/resize/375x500!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F07%2F04%2F596bfbea4b65b90cf8621f5ca646%2Flokosh-photo.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSI1MDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjM3NXB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The poet Lokosh penned the libretto.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The second movement describes the life of Chickasaws in their ancestral homeland in what’s now the American South, near the Mississippi and the Tombigbee Rivers, before the tribe’s forcible removal to the Indian Territory on the Trail of Tears. The third movement describes the obligations of living Chickasaw people to their ancestors and asserts their continued existence.</p><p>The Chicago-based EcoVoice Project produces projects relating music to the natural world, with the aim of promoting sustainability and stewardship for the planet. Previous concerts, often celebrating solstices or Earth Day, have included Sarah Kirkland Snider’s popular “Mass for the Endangered” and works by the environmental-activist-turned-composer John Luther Adams from his “Music and the Anthropocene.”</p><p>The composer of “Ámmo'naka,” Jerod Impichchaachaaha' Tate, works with musical elements drawn from his Chickasaw ancestry, using tools of modern concert music. “When I’m composing I am literally transported into our homelands in the deep woods of Mississippi,” he said. “I feel very connected to the elements every time I’m composing, so the trees, the animals, the weather, the sun, thunderstorms — the environment is a very big part of my ethos.”</p><p>Tate aims to create a musical sphere where the specific Chickasaw story can express the relationship of all humanity to Earth. “All of our ancient cultures are beautifully rooted in our environments,” he said, “and so I want music that resonates to all people who have a sense of homeland.”</p><p>Lokosh agrees. The Chickasaw worldview harmonizes with EcoVoice’s message of environmental stewardship. “In our traditional worldview, there’s no hierarchy,” he said. “I’m no better or worse than a deer or a turtle. When we hunt, you’re killing a relative. It’s not a prey animal, it’s not a game animal — it’s just like you are.”</p><p>“Ámmo'naka” itself is also an act of conservation — not ecological, but linguistic. It’s unusual to create new artistic works in Chickasaw, although not unprecedented: Tate and Lokosh collaborated on the opera “Loksi' Shaali'” (“Shell Shaker”), the first opera ever presented in an American Indian language when it premiered in 2022.</p><p>Lokosh said that even though only 30 or so native Chickasaw speakers remain, multiple ways of promoting the language are underway, such as an immersive day care for very young children, video Chickasaw lessons and the construction of new terms for modern technologies. “Poetry is another form of life for the language,” he said. “We’re pushing for a vitality or return to a time when the language was fully socialized.”</p><p>At the end of the piece, big, open chords similar to those at the opening return. The text translates as “This is the way that we shall be,” the sopranos are back in the stratosphere, and it’s loud. One final shout and the piece ends. The crayfish and the buzzard have done their work, and the creation has emerged into the world.<br></p>
entries.author: Graham Meyer | WBEZ

entries.title: Ex-Mayor Lori Lightfoot's chief operating officer is at the center of City Hall intern, contracting scandal
entries.summary: <p>When Lori Lightfoot was Chicago’s mayor, City Hall put out a press release in 2021 touting the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2021/5/24/22451876/lightfoot-chief-of-staff-maurice-classen-leaves-replaced-by-sybil-madison-chicago-mayor-city-hall" >hiring of Paul Goodrich</a> as her new chief operating officer, a multi-faceted role involving “the oversight, development and implementation of strategic mayoral initiatives and policy priorities for Chicago’s infrastructure, transportation, regulatory and municipal administrative services.”</p><p>Lightfoot was quoted in the media statement as saying: "For years, Paul dedicated his career to making businesses and organizations work more efficiently.”</p><p>Five years later, Goodrich is referenced in another city document in less-flattering circumstances — as someone who allegedly used his position as a top city official under Lightfoot to secretly get his son a paid internship with a city contractor, and then tried to get that contractor nearly $10 million more in payments from taxpayers that the company may not have been entitled to.</p><p>This was all laid out in a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/04/15/inspector-general-deborah-witzburg-report-ex-mayoral-staffer-city-contractor-unauthorized-work-investigation" >report released</a> last week by City Hall’s in-house watchdog, Inspector General Deborah Witzburg, but without specific names and titles.</p><p>The Chicago Sun-Times has since confirmed from sources that Goodrich is the city official referenced in the document — and the city contractor is a company run by Chicago businessman Robert Blackwell Jr., who’s been a friend and campaign contributor to former President Barack Obama.</p><p>Witzburg declined to comment. Goodrich and Blackwell didn’t responded to requests for comment.</p><p>The revelations have not only proved embarrassing for Lightfoot, who always framed herself as a reformer, but also her successor, Mayor Brandon Johnson.</p><p>Blackwell’s firm, EKI-Digital, has remained a city contractor under Johnson. Records show the company has worked for City Hall since the early 2000s when Mayor Richard M. Daley was in office.</p><p>A spokesperson for Johnson said that Goodrich directed Blackwell’s company to perform work in March and April 2023 that wasn’t part of his contract with the city’s Department of Technology and Innovation. </p><p>To avoid costly litigation, the city entered into a settlement agreement with Blackwell, the spokesperson said. He added, “Consequently, the Johnson administration implemented new policies to prevent vendors from performing non-contracted work.’’</p><p>City Hall has continued paying EKI for consulting services it has done under a separate contract with the technology department.</p><p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-bd0000" name="image-bd0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e68b01c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1256x705+0+246/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F65%2Fa0%2F7f7fe9a242f1a0a851c4270dd262%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-21-at-12-53-33-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4d9e327/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1256x705+0+246/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F65%2Fa0%2F7f7fe9a242f1a0a851c4270dd262%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-21-at-12-53-33-pm.png width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bb1bd6b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1256x705+0+246/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F65%2Fa0%2F7f7fe9a242f1a0a851c4270dd262%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-21-at-12-53-33-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A 2021 press release from City Hall on Paul Goodrich's hiring." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bb1bd6b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1256x705+0+246/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F65%2Fa0%2F7f7fe9a242f1a0a851c4270dd262%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-21-at-12-53-33-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dcf05bc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1256x705+0+246/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F65%2Fa0%2F7f7fe9a242f1a0a851c4270dd262%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-21-at-12-53-33-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bb1bd6b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1256x705+0+246/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F65%2Fa0%2F7f7fe9a242f1a0a851c4270dd262%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-21-at-12-53-33-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A 2021 press release from City Hall on Paul Goodrich’s hiring.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>City of Chicago</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Last week, Johnson pointed a finger squarely back at Lightfoot, saying the problems involved a “previous mayoral administration,” while a spokesperson for Lightfoot said she “had not been previously made aware of this investigation, and has not received any communication” from the inspector general’s office.</p><p>“The allegations in question must be treated with the utmost seriousness.”</p><p>Goodrich’s son’s LinkedIn page states he was an intern with EKI-Digital from June 2022 to May 2023, the month Johnson was sworn in after handily defeating Lightfoot in her reelection effort.</p><p>Goodrich is on the board of Steppenwolf — one of Chicago’s premier theater ensembles whose actors have included Gary Sinise, John Malkovich, Laurie Metcalf and Martha Plimpton — along with Lightfoot’s wife Amy Eshleman.</p><p>Prior to joining Lightfoot’s staff as the chief operating officer, Goodrich spent two years as a senior business adviser to Choose Chicago, the city’s convention and tourism bureau.</p><p>Witzburg would not say whether she referred the case to federal authorities. Her report said her office’s investigation determined that “a former senior staff member in the Mayor’s Office used their City title and its authority to solicit a job for their child from the president of a City contractor.”</p><p>“The president of the City contractor hired the senior staff member’s child for a paid internship reporting directly to the president. While their child worked for the contractor, the senior staff member attempted to increase the scope of the contractor’s work for the City, allowed the contractor to perform unauthorized work for the City, and attempted to facilitate $9.6 million in payments to the contractor to which the contractor was not entitled.”</p><p>The inspector general’s office also “found that the $9.6 million in invoices the contractor submitted to the City contained false claims and intentional and negligent billing irregularities, and the invoices failed to comply with City procurement procedures. OIG’s investigation revealed that the senior staff member and the president of the contractor had communicated with [City Hall’s law department] regarding the invoices.”</p><p>On Witzburg’s advice, the mayor’s spokesperson said the procurement services department has “initiated disbarment proceedings” that could preclude Blackwell from landing any more work from the city.</p><p> </p><p>Another Blackwell business, Killerspin LLC, donated $80,000 to a campaign fund benefiting Lightfoot in 2022, records show.</p>
entries.author: Tom Schuba

entries.title: How has the Black Chicago accent retained its Southern roots?
entries.summary: <p>I’m from the South Side of Chicago. And when I’m on the radio or on a podcast, you can absolutely tell. When I’m answering a Curious City question and I’m trying to say that a listener asked a question, it sometimes comes out sounding like “ax.” That’s something linguists call metathesis: Sounds in a word swap places, and it’s common in Southern speech.</p><p>But I didn’t grow up in the southern part of the country. I’m two generations removed from Greenwood, Mississippi, on my mother’s side and Sunflower, Mississippi, on my father’s. And yet, when I fully relax into how I naturally speak, I can sound pretty Southern.</p><p>I’m not an outlier. Many Black Chicagoans talk just like I do. That led a Curious City listener to ask: <i>Why have Black Chicagoans kept so many features of Southern dialect?</i></p><p>This answer goes back to the Great Migration, roughly between 1915 and 1970, during which a half-million Black people moved from Southern states to Chicago, creating a Black Chicago English you can still hear in the dialect of their children, their grandchildren and great-grandchildren.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e20000" name="image-e20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a2f4fe6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2819x1582+0+393/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0a%2Fef%2Ffaf785484463886523b1c37dfcd5%2Fgreat-migration-family.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A family that arrived in Chicago from the rural South.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture, Jean Blackwell Hutson Research and Reference Division, The New York Public Library. (1922)</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Hearing the Black Chicago accent</h3><p>How Black Chicagoans speak is a variation of African American English, which also has Southern roots.</p><p>“It's partly the language that comes from descendants of enslaved peoples,” said Sharese King, one of two researchers leading the Chicagoland Language Project. “That is why you see a lot of overlap even between African American English and Southern varieties of English.”</p><p>As part of this project, King has been collecting data on how we, today’s Black Chicagoans, speak. In her research, many participants talk just like I do. Let’s take the word “car” — I naturally pronounce it “cah.” King says that “r-lessness” comes up a lot in her interviews and is “a hallmark of the Black Chicagoan dialect.”</p><p>Another attribute often found in Black Chicago speech is monophthongization: When we say a double vowel, also known as a diphthong, it sounds like just one. So Black Chicagoans will often say a word like “I,” which often sounds like “ai,” as just “ah.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fa0000" name="image-fa0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f740364/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3756x2108+0+153/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faa%2Fd6%2F2d85c9b8422e8cb64f25d0915d15%2Fchoral-society.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A choral group poses for a photo in Chicago. Between 1915 and 1970, about a half-million Black people moved from Southern states to Chicago, creating a Black Chicago English you can still hear in the dialect today.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture, Jean Blackwell Hutson Research and Reference Division, The New York Public Library. (1922)</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>King notes while there are strong patterns, more research is needed. Black Chicagoans might not be the only people who have these aspects of speech.</p><p>“It is likely that both the r-lessness and the monophthongization present different in Black Chicago English based on technical details we haven't measured yet (e.g. where the nucleus of the vowel is located in the mouth or the environment where it more likely deletes),” King said. “But it's hard to say without having reached the stage of measuring these vowels using our fancy software. That project is on the docket, though.”</p><h3><b>A connection to the South</b></h3><p>But why has the Southern accent remained so prevalent? King offers three reasons.</p><p>First, when it comes to Chicago, segregation has actually preserved those Southern aspects of speech. A 2023 <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/race-class-communities/2023/06/19/chicago-remains-the-most-segregated-big-city-in-america" target="_blank" ><u>WBEZ analysis</u></a> shows Chicago is still the most segregated city in America. And although many Black Chicagoans are generations removed from Great Migration elders, we are more likely to still live in neighborhoods with each other.</p><p>King says that proximity within the group matters.</p><p>“One of the things that is really important about maintaining any kind of dialect or language variety is the idea that you're in close, close-up proximity with a particular group of people,” she said.</p><p>The second reason is that while Black families may have moved to Chicago, many never lost connection with relatives who stayed in the South, and our accents stay as strong as those relationships. Summer trips to visit family in Mississippi or Louisiana, for example, remain a commonality.</p><p>“You also still have people who express so many connections still to their family who are not up North or who are not in the Midwest,” King said.</p><p>And the last reason: We like how we talk. We like ourselves. We’re not trying to emulate other people’s speeches because the people who we see as cool are also Black Chicagoans.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rapper and actor Common looks into the camera while sitting at the Jay Pritzker Pavilion at Millennium Park, Friday, June 14, 2024.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“People are not always aware in their linguistic performance that they're drawing on or what exact features they're drawing on,” King said. “Sometimes, we like to sound like people we like.”</p><p>So if I end up performing an accent, it’s that of people I like. I might want to sound like Common when he leans into his love for Chicago in an interview, or like a girl from my high school who always had the coolest confidence, or like Chicago kids that pop up on my TikTok “For You” page.</p><p>And for me, as a Black Chicagoan who at one point thought I’d have to change how I spoke to be a journalist, King’s research is comforting. It’s a reminder that how I speak means connection. It means history, family and community. And those are things I never want to lose.</p><p><i>Arionne Nettles is a journalism professor, culture reporter and audio aficionado. She is the author of “We Are the Culture: Black Chicago’s Influence on Everything” and “Journalism for Dummies.” Follow her </i><a class="Link" href="http://twitter.com/arionnenettles" target="_blank" ><i><u>@arionnenettles</u></i></a><i>.</i></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-750000" name="html-embed-module-750000"></a><script async src="https://modules.wearehearken.com/wbez/embed/4190.js"></script> </div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Arionne Nettles

entries.title: Former Schaumburg village employee charged in downstate predator sting
entries.summary: <p>A village of Schaumburg employee and resident resigned last Thursday after being arrested in an operation conducted by the downstate Woodford County Sheriff’s Office Proactive Unit’s Predator Apprehension Team.</p><p>Brian Rafferty, 41, who had been the Schaumburg’s Fleet Division Manager for the past nine years, is accused of traveling to Germantown Hills — about 8 miles northeast of Peoria — to meet who he believed was a 15-year-old girl, according to the sheriff’s office.</p><p>He was, in fact, communicating with law enforcement officers from Woodford County posing as a teen, authorities allege.</p><p>Following his arrest April 13, Rafferty was charged with indecent solicitation of a child and grooming, officials said. He was granted pretrial release and his next court hearing is scheduled for May 11 in Woodford County.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260421/crime/schaumburg-village-employee-charged-in-downstate-predator-sting/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com</a>.<br></p>
entries.author: Daily Herald

entries.title: Suspect's lawyer alerts cops to woman strangled to death in Greater Grand Crossing apartment
entries.summary: <p>A man is being questioned by police after his lawyer alerted police that a woman had been strangled and his client wanted to turn himself in after the Monday killing in a Greater Grand Crossing apartment Monday night, according to police. </p><p>No charges have been announced by police, who are interviewing a person of interest as of Wednesday morning, police said. </p><p>A Tuesday autopsy determined Terriyanna Johnson, 29, who was found around 8:40 p.m. Monday inside an apartment at 524 E. 72nd St., died of strangulation and her death was ruled a homicide, according to the Cook County medical examiner's office. </p><p>Police learned of Johnson's killing when they got a call from a criminal defense attorney who said there was a dead person at the location and his client wanted to turn himself in, according to police reports obtained by the Sun-Times. </p><p>First responders forced themselves into the home and found Johnson on the bedroom floor covered in a blanket, officials said. A small amount of blood was found near her head and she had a bruise on her upper left arm, according to the reports. </p><p>Johnson was pronounced dead at the scene at 10:01 p.m. Monday, authorities said. </p><p>Area 1 detectives are investigating.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-i/" target="_blank" >Lightfoot’s chief operating officer is at center of City Hall scandal</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-d/" target="_blank" >Tim Novak</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-h/" target="_blank" >Robert Herguth</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-k/" target="_blank" >Fran Spielman</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-u/" target="_blank" >Tom Schuba</a></h5><p><b>Contracting scandal</b>: Paul Goodrich, former Mayor Lori Lightfoot's chief operating officer, allegedly used his position to secretly get his son a paid internship with a city contractor, and then tried to get that contractor nearly $10 million more in payments from taxpayers to which the company may not have been entitled.</p><p><b>Sun-Times investigates</b>: Allegations lodged against Goodrich were all laid out in a <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-o/" target="_blank" >report released</a> last week by City Hall’s in-house watchdog, Inspector General Deborah Witzburg, but without specific names and titles. The Sun-Times has since confirmed from sources that Goodrich is the official referenced in the document, and that the contractor company is run by Chicago businessman Robert Blackwell Jr., a friend and campaign contributor to former President Barack Obama.</p><p><b>At issue</b>: The revelations have proved embarrassing not only for Lightfoot, who always framed herself as a reformer, but also for her successor, Mayor Brandon Johnson. Blackwell’s firm, EKI-Digital, has remained a city contractor under Johnson. Records show the company has worked for City Hall since the early 2000s, when Mayor Richard M. Daley was in office.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-b/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>MORE ON CITY HALL 🏛️</h4><div This year, some parents say they remain frustrated despite the Park District’s changes.</p><p><b>City's efforts</b>: The Park District says it has implemented new methods to address these issues, including a revamped registration landing page online with "high demand" icons to label programs likely to sell out and webinars to answer questions before registration.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-a/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><h4></h4><p><b>Have you registered your child for a Chicago Park District summer camp program? The libretto with original poetry written in Chickasaw by the poet Lokosh tells the traditional Chickasaw story about the creation of the world and the relationship between humans and the land. "Ámmo’naka poshki" means "In a beginning / Our Mother." Composer Jerod Impichchaachaaha’ Tate said he worked with musical elements drawn from his ancestry, using tools of modern concert music.</p><p><b>Key quote</b>: Tate says the Chickasaw story can express the relationship of all humanity to Earth. "All of our ancient cultures are beautifully rooted in our environments ... I want music that resonates to all people who have a sense of homeland."</p><p><b>If you go</b>: Catch the performance 7:30 p.m. Wednesday at the Mundelein Center for the Fine and Performing Arts at Loyola University, 1020 W. Sheridan Road. Tickets are $5–$15</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-yh/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>ON WBEZ 91.5 FM 📻</h4><h4></h4><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-yu/" target="_blank" ><b>In the Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons, 9 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-d8aa59d4-3e4e-11f1-a596-b39fc3ff13c4"><li><b>Earth Day every day</b>: Guests Nitya Jakka of Chicago's Eat for Impact initiative, Seth Magle of the Urban Wildlife Institute and Gina Roxas of the Trickster Cultural Center explain how individuals can make meaningful change against massive threats to our planet and nurture new habits to keep the Earth Day spirit alive beyond April 22.<br></li><li><b>Visiting The Land School</b>: Afton Battle of The Land School and Rebuild Foundation gives a tour of the new artist incubator and third space in the Greater Grand Crossing neighborhood.<br></li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-jl/" target="_blank" ><b>Say More with Mary Dixon and Patrick Smith, 10 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-d8aa80e1-3e4e-11f1-a596-b39fc3ff13c4"><li><b>Earth's future</b>: Guests Karen Weigert of Loyola University and Shari Pundrich of Remade in Chicago discuss local efforts and scientific advances impacting the planet's future. Her first 20 stops offered her only one role: Dishwasher.</p><p>The young chef finally landed at the corner of Illinois and Clark streets, which for nearly 40 years has been the headquarters of chef Rick Bayless’ ever-growing culinary empire. In a decade, Jacobs, 29, has risen from an unpaid stage at the fast-casual XOCO to co-chef of Frontera Grill.</p><p>The Harvard, Illinois, native is now redefining what vegetable-forward dining looks like at one of Chicago’s longest-standing destination restaurants. And her skills are gaining wider recognition: She won the Food Network’s competition show "Chopped" in 2023 and, more recently, a Banchet Award for Chicago’s Rising Chef of the Year.</p><p>Jacobs incorporates vegetable dishes into Frontera’s regional Mexican menu, which has long been heavy on pork, chicken and beef.</p><p>Recalling last fall's Banchet Awards, she said: "I heard my name, and I just had like a flashback of me going to those restaurants, knocking on those doors, having this passion of just cooking and making it in Chicago ... And in that moment, I just saw all the years and all the hard work flash before my eyes."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-tl/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wikkuuk-l-ty/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: El director de Whitney Young, baleado mientras conducía, dice que está 'agradecido de estar vivo'
entries.summary: <p>El director de la escuela secundaria Whitney Young, Rickey Harris, escapó ileso después de que le dispararan el viernes por la noche mientras conducía por el vecindario de Washington Heights en el lado sur de Chicago.</p><p>Harris, de 53 años, viajaba en dirección sur cuando unos disparos alcanzaron su vehículo a eso de las 10:15 p.m. del viernes en la cuadra 1300 al oeste de 95th Street, según la Policía de Chicago.</p><p>"Lo estoy procesando. Es una situación día a día", dijo Harris al Sun-Times. "Pero ciertamente estoy agradecido de estar vivo".</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-290000" name="module-290000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>La policía está investigando el incidente y no ha anunciado ninguna detención.</p><p>Harris mencionó que no cree que él fuera el objetivo. Dijo que ha visto un video que muestra al tirador apuntando a otro automóvil cercano.</p><p>"Dios es real y Dios es bueno", dijo sobre salir físicamente ileso.</p><p>También ha buscado asesoramiento profesional de salud mental para lidiar con las "subidas y bajadas" emocionales tras el tiroteo, comentó. Harris alentó a otros a buscar también asesoramiento si han experimentado traumas.</p><p>"Ellos pueden ayudar a comprender lo que sucedió y lo que estoy viviendo", dijo Harris. "Incluso más allá de esta semana, este mes, este año".</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los orificios de bala acribillan el lado lateral del auto de Rickey Harris, director de la secundaria Whitney Young, por debajo de las ventanillas rotas que también fueron alcanzadas por los disparos el viernes.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Proporcionada</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>El residente de Chicago de toda la vida, que ha trabajado en educación durante casi 30 años, dijo que nunca había experimentado algo así. Harris destacó la necesidad de más programas para jóvenes que los mantengan alejados de las calles. Eso incluye hacer de las escuelas un lugar seguro donde los estudiantes se sientan cómodos hablando sobre sus luchas y necesidades, añadió.</p><p>Harris elogió esfuerzos como una reciente "toma de padres" en respuesta a una supuesta toma de adolescentes en Hyde Park que nunca se materializó. Pero alentó a los adultos a involucrar a los jóvenes en conversaciones sobre políticas porque ellos "responden a la autenticidad".</p><p>Agregó que los niños deben ser responsables por sus acciones.</p><p>"Los estudiantes tienen que sentirse valorados, y tenemos que crear espacios donde se cultive un propósito", dijo Harris. "Parte de lo que estos jóvenes están pasando es una falta de esperanza. No valoran la vida ni tienen un sentido de propósito. Por lo tanto, no significa nada que esta persona esté afuera bajo la lluvia a las 10:30 de la noche disparando a los autos al azar. Algo tiene que hacerse. Estamos en un estado grave".</p><p>Espero que mi experiencia impulse conversaciones sobre invertir en recursos de salud mental, promulgar leyes de armas más estrictas y corregir "años de daño causado en ciertas comunidades", comentó.</p><p>Las comunidades también deben estar más unidas y cuidarse mutuamente, dijo Harris. Quiere que las personas piensen en algo que puedan hacer para conectar con un joven.</p><p>"Este momento es más grande que lo que me pasó. No dejemos que esto sea solo otra historia", dijo Harris. "En nuestras diferentes capacidades, todos tenemos un papel que desempeñar... Espero que surja algo de esto en términos de un cambio real".</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: Inicia la construcción de un campus industrial de 4 edificios en Back of the Yards
entries.summary: <p>Back of the Yards tiene una larga historia industrial, siendo el hogar del antiguo Union Stock Yard y de una variedad de líneas de ferrrocarril.</p><p>La firma de bienes raíces Brookfield Properties está impulsando la próxima ola de crecimiento industrial del vecindario con su mayor proyecto industrial activo en Chicago.</p><p>El viernes, líderes de la ciudad y del proyecto se reunieron para inaugurar Western Works, un proyecto industrial de $104 millones en el lado sur.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b30000" name="module-b30000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>El proyecto, ubicado en 4435 S. Western Blvd., incluye cuatro edificios que van de 75,000 a 198,000 pies cuadrados y traerá cientos de empleos temporales y permanentes.</p><p>Los líderes presentan Western Works como el último proyecto que mantiene a Chicago como una potencia nacional en logística y manufactura. La conectividad de la ciudad con las principales vías ferroviarias, el transporte aéreo de carga y otras rutas de transporte la han convertido en un centro para la logística y otras industrias relacionadas.</p><p>El alcalde Brandon Johnson dijo que el proyecto es "otra señal de que se están realizando inversiones en esta gran ciudad" y que "las oportunidades están creciendo".</p><p>"Con este inicio de obras, declaramos con orgullo que el lado suroeste de Chicago está abierto a hacer negocio", dijo.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" Western Blvd. se convertirá en el hogar de Western Works, un campus industrial de cuatro edificios en Back of the Yards." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e069b2a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+328/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2F83%2F1940c10e40b0bd04e9466531d194%2Fwestern-260420-17.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a6dfffb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+328/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2F83%2F1940c10e40b0bd04e9466531d194%2Fwestern-260420-17.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e069b2a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+328/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2F83%2F1940c10e40b0bd04e9466531d194%2Fwestern-260420-17.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>El sitio de construcción en 4435 S. Western Blvd. se convertirá en el hogar de Western Works, un campus industrial de cuatro edificios en Back of the Yards.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Brookfield Properties, con sede en Nueva York y cuya división minorista está ubicada en Chicago, tiene un portafolio que incluye propiedades de oficinas, ventas al menudeo, apartamentos, hospitales y logística. Algunas de sus propiedades locales incluyen el Oakbrook Center y el Renaissance Chicago Downtown Hotel.</p><p>La firma cuenta con aproximadamente 30 propiedades industriales en el área metropolitana de Chicago, según Turner Cook, vicepresidente de desarrollo en Brookfield.</p><p>Los cuatro edificios en Western Works serán instalaciones de última generación, manteniendo al mismo tiempo la estética de Western Boulevard, dijo Cook. Los edificios tendrán fachadas de ladrillo que dan a la calle para integrarse en el carácter del corredor.</p><p>Las propiedades se están construyendo especulativamente, lo que significa que no hay inquilinos asegurados. Cook dijo que cada edificio podría albergar hasta 10 usuarios, que pueden variar desde manufactura, producción de alimentos, almacenamiento, distribución o algún otro uso industrial ligero.</p><p>El campus tendrá 87 muelles de carga y 635 espacios de estacionamiento. Se espera que la construcción finalice en 2027. Según la Municipalidad, el proyecto anticipa crear 150 empleos de construcción y 100 posiciones permanentes a tiempo completo.</p><p>Brookfield adquirió el sitio de 28 acres en 2021 de Wheatland Tube, un fabricante de tubos, conductos eléctricos y otros productos, por $24.4 millones, según muestran los registros de propiedad. Wheatland, parte de Zekelman Industries con sede en Chicago, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/4/17/23683570/warehouse-space-logistics-center-south-side-wheatland-tube-factory" target="_blank" >transferió la propiedad</a> de sus tres edificios a Brookfield en una venta-arrendamiento que permitió a Wheatland permanecer hasta encontrar un nuevo hogar.</p><div class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Brookfield ya ha demolido los antiguos edificios de Wheatland y se están realizando trabajos de excavación. La construcción comenzará después de la inauguración del viernes, según la firma de bienes raíces. También se ha iniciado el prearrendamiento.</p><p>La concejal Julia Ramírez (12º) vive frente al proyecto en Western Boulevard. Creció caminando entre los antiguos edificios de Western Works, preguntándose para qué servían. Aún ahora, dijo que es difícil resumir lo que significa el corredor industrial en su distrito para la ciudad.</p><p>“Son un vínculo y una conexión con nuestro mercado”, dijo Ramírez. “Es una de las fortalezas de la ciudad, de este vecindario y de las personas que trabajan entre ellos”.</p><p>El sitio de Western Works está en el histórico Central Manufacturing District de la ciudad, que a menudo se menciona como el <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/morning-shift/2019/09/06/whats-that-building-chicagos-central-manufacturing-district" target="_blank" >primer distrito de manufactura planificado</a> o parque industrial del país. También está en el corredor industrial de Stockyards, con acceso directo a múltiples interestatales y al transporte público.</p><p>La comisionada del Departamento de Planificación y Desarrollo, Ciere Boatright, dijo que Western Works es "un gran negocio" y una inversión importante.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a Properties | Proporcionada</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>La ciudad ha sido un foco industrial después de que la pandemia de COVID-19 acelerara las compras en línea y las necesidades de logística. Datos recientes del primer trimestre de la firma de bienes raíces comerciales, Avison Young, revelaron que la disponibilidad de edificios industriales se ha vuelto cada vez más limitada en la región de Chicago, una señal de la continua alta demanda. La tasa de vacantes general se sitúa en 6.1%, con una disponibilidad para "usuarios de grandes superficies" que necesitan más de 500,000 pies cuadrados de espacio en 7.2%.</p><p>"Este proyecto de $104 millones es una gran muestra de confianza en el mercado de Chicago", dijo Boatright. "Quiero felicitar a Brookfield por su compromiso con este sitio, el corredor industrial de Stockyards y el legado de la ciudad como un centro de transporte y distribución".</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Feds plan to use protest chants to prosecute Broadview ICE protesters — but balk at free speech defense
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors say the remaining members of the “Broadview Six” should be barred from making arguments about free speech rights when they go to trial<b> </b>next month — though the feds also plan to use protest chants caught on video as evidence against the group.</p><p>Prosecutors also argued there should be no references to the October<b> </b>shooting<b> </b>of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/03/marimar-martinez-shot-border-patrol-chicago-speaks-out-after-good-pretti-deaths-i-am-their-voice" target="_blank" >Marimar Martinez</a> by a Border Patrol agent in Chicago, nor of the fatal shootings<b> </b>this year of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by immigration authorities in Minnesota.</p><p>Meanwhile, defense attorneys asked<b> </b>U.S. District Judge April Perry to bar the feds from referring to their clients as “rioters,”<b> </b>“Antifa members”<b> </b>or<b> </b>“domestic terrorists.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/16/chicago-broadview-six-kat-abughazaleh-midway-blitz-trump-ice" >Remaining ‘Broadview Six’ defendants want conspiracy charge tossed, argue protesting isn’t a crime</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>“They are inflammatory terms that carry strong political, social, and emotional connotations that risk unfairly biasing the jury against the defendants,”<b> </b>the defense attorneys wrote.</p><p>The arguments appeared in a series of motions filed Tuesday, five weeks ahead of the second federal trial to result from Operation Midway Blitz. Four people, including former congressional candidate Kat<b> </b>Abughazaleh, are <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/10/29/kat-abughazaleh-conspiracy-indictment-broadview-protests-donald-trump-deportation-campaign" target="_blank" >accused of a conspiracy</a> to impede a federal agent.</p><p>Also charged are Oak Park village trustee Brian<b> </b>Straw, 45th Ward Democratic committeeperson Michael<b> </b>Rabbitt<b> </b>and<b> </b>Andre<b> </b>Martin, a member of Abughazaleh’s campaign staff. The feds earlier this year <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-trump-kat-abughazaleh-sharp-joselyn-walsh-broadview-six" target="_blank" >dropped charges against two others</a> originally indicted in the case.</p><p>Questions about free speech have swirled since a grand jury handed up that indictment in October, in the heat of the Trump administration’s aggressive deportation campaign last fall.<b> </b>Perry is still considering a defense request that she toss the conspiracy charge <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/16/chicago-broadview-six-kat-abughazaleh-midway-blitz-trump-ice" target="_blank" >based on the First Amendment</a>, which protects the freedom of speech, religion, assembly and the press. </p><p>Lawyers are next due back in Perry’s courtroom<b> </b>May 13. The trial is set to begin May 26.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d20000" name="image-d20000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bbe1361/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2286x1283+0+61/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2Fc8%2F7efede6e41d599881f57e7274e25%2Fkatscreenshot2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9424784/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2286x1283+0+61/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2Fc8%2F7efede6e41d599881f57e7274e25%2Fkatscreenshot2.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aeeca55/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2286x1283+0+61/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2Fc8%2F7efede6e41d599881f57e7274e25%2Fkatscreenshot2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Kat Abughazaleh appears in this screenshot of footage from outside an immigration holding facility in Broadview. Abughazaleh and three others are charged in a conspiracy to impede a federal agent outside the facility." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aeeca55/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2286x1283+0+61/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2Fc8%2F7efede6e41d599881f57e7274e25%2Fkatscreenshot2.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ff8ef2d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2286x1283+0+61/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2Fc8%2F7efede6e41d599881f57e7274e25%2Fkatscreenshot2.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aeeca55/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2286x1283+0+61/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe9%2Fc8%2F7efede6e41d599881f57e7274e25%2Fkatscreenshot2.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Kat Abughazaleh appears in this screenshot of footage from outside an immigration holding facility in Broadview. Abughazaleh and three others are charged in a conspiracy to impede a federal agent outside the facility.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>All four of the remaining defendants are involved in local Democratic politics. U.S. Attorney Andrew Boutros <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/07/chicago-crime-justice-department-doj-boutros-trump-midway-blitz-corruption" target="_blank" >insisted in an interview</a> this month that politics have played no role in charging decisions made by his office.</p><p>Regardless, the feds have struggled to secure convictions against people charged with nonimmigration crimes tied to Midway Blitz. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >Out of 32 known defendants</a>, one person has pleaded guilty. Twenty others have been cleared, and four others are on track to have their charges dismissed.</p><p>In the only other trial to result from Midway Blitz, a jury<b> </b>in January<b> </b><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/22/federal-jury-rejects-bovino-murder-plot-after-called-upon-stand-up-overreaching-government" target="_blank" >acquitted<b> </b>a man</a><b> </b>accused of a murder-for-hire plot aimed at then-U.S. Border Patrol Cmdr. Gregory Bovino.</p><p>Now a new jury could soon hear evidence revolving around events on the morning of Sept. 26, outside the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement<b> </b>facility in Broadview. The four defendants are accused of joining a group<b> </b>that surrounded<b> </b>an<b> </b>ICE<b> </b>agent’s vehicle<b> </b>and pushed, scratched and otherwise damaged it.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-midway-blitz-immigration-trump-kat-abughazaleh-sharp-joselyn-walsh-broadview-six" >Broadview 4? Feds drop conspiracy charges against 2 ICE protesters, including Dem who ended campaign</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Prosecutors say they plan to introduce “anti-ICE statements and chants”<b> </b>by the defendants and their co-conspirators heard by Broadview police and caught on video. They also said Broadview police heard the protesters discuss not letting “ICE cars through to their facility.”</p><p>“Such evidence will be introduced to show defendants’ motivation and purpose in joining the conspiracy,” Assistant U.S. Attorney William<b> </b>Hogan<b> </b>wrote Tuesday. “It demonstrates that the co-conspirators swarmed and impeded [the agent’s] vehicle because of his affiliation with ICE.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatLeft> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c80000" name="image-c80000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
