2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d36bfd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F35%2Ff5%2Fabe4ba2b44f1b2989a0f9c14d81f%2Fbviewsix-260320-05.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Oak Park Village Trustee Brian Straw leaves the Dirksen Federal Courthouse after a hearing in the so-called “Broadview Six” case on Thursday, March 19, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Hogan wrote that defense attorneys should not be allowed to argue entrapment or seek jury nullification — a scenario in which jurors acquit the defendants even if prosecutors have proven their case. The prosecutor argued that<b> </b>references to the shootings of Martinez, Good and Pretti “would clearly invite jury nullification.”</p><p>Finally, he argued that Perry should be the jury’s “sole source of information on the law and the contours of the First Amendment.”</p><p>“There is no ‘protest immunity’ such that force or intimidation are shielded from prosecution by the First Amendment, and the defendants should not be allowed to improperly suggest that the jury can find, or consider, that they enjoy an immunity from prosecution under the Constitution where none exists,” Hogan wrote.</p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Marimar Martinez, shot by CBP agent, testifies, urges accountability or 'more people are going to get hurt'
entries.summary: <p>Marimar Martinez, the Chicago woman who was shot by a Border Patrol agent in October, testified Wednesday before the Homeland Security Committee in Washington, warning federal lawmakers that if immigration agents aren’t held accountable, “more people are going to get hurt.”</p><p>Martinez, 31, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/03/marimar-martinez-shot-border-patrol-chicago-speaks-out-after-good-pretti-deaths-i-am-their-voice" target="_blank" ><u>survived being shot</u></a> five times by Border Patrol agent Charles Exum after a car carrying three CBP agents collided with her vehicle Oct. 4 near 39th Street and Kedzie Avenue.</p><p>She said she was driving to donate clothes to a church that morning when she noticed an SUV that seemed to be driven by federal immigration agents who “had been invading my Hispanic neighborhood in recent weeks.”</p><p>After seeing uniformed Border Patrol agents in the car, Martinez said she followed the SUV, honked and shouted, “La migra,” to alert her neighbors of their presence.</p><p>Her car remained “two to three feet” to the left of the CBP vehicle when Exum veered into her lane. She made eye contact with Exum and “watched as he turned his steering wheel once again to his left and sideswiped my vehicle,” she said.</p><p>“I immediately froze, slammed on my brakes and stopped my car. The Border Patrol stopped just one to two car lengths ahead of me,” Martinez said. “At that moment, I believed I was in danger based on what I knew watching the news coverage of ICE agents … in Chicago.”</p><p>She drove forward, around the CBP agents as one agent pointed a gun at her, she said. She continued veering left until she struck a curb.</p><p>“I felt burning sensations in my arms and legs. Shortly after, I began to feel sharp impacts and burning sensation as I continued to drive past the Border Patrol agents. I could hear my back passenger window shatter,” Martinez said. “I looked down and noticed blood gushing out of my arms and legs and realized I had been shot multiple times.”</p><p>Wednesday was the first time <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/04/16/marimar-martinez-border-patrol-shooting-victim-chicago-congress-testimony" target="_blank" ><u>Martinez</u></a> testified before a committee. She was joined by the Rev. David Black, of the First Presbyterian Church of Chicago, who testified about when ICE agents fired pepper balls at him as he prayed outside the ICE processing facility in Broadview.</p><p>“What was done to me is only a reflection of what is being done to people in my community who have no culprit and no platform,” Black said. “On a daily basis, I witnessed the fear and the suffering of those who are being targeted by this violent campaign.”</p><p>George Retes Jr., a U.S. citizen who was tear gassed and arrested by ICE while on his way to work in southern California last July, and Ryan Ecklund, another U.S. citizen who was detained by ICE in January in Minnesota, also testified at Wednesday’s hearing.</p><p>Martinez first appeared before Congress at a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/03/marimar-martinez-goes-to-washington-wants-trump-officials-to-admit-not-a-domestic-terrorist" target="_blank" ><u>Feb. 3 public forum</u></a> organized in response to the deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti at the hands of federal immigration officers in Minnesota. She told lawmakers then that she wanted federal agents held accountable, wanted to hear an apology and wanted the Trump administration to acknowledge she is “not a domestic terrorist.”</p><p>She reiterated that Wednesday, mainly asserting that she and “us Americans” want honesty, transparency and accountability from the Trump administration.</p><p>“Look at what happened to Renee [Good], look at what happened to Alex Pretti. It’s bound to happen sooner or later if we don’t hold these agents accountable for their actions. More people are going to get hurt,” Martinez said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="150" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/xmg8n9mcvzA?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Funding Lapse & Security Gaps: Assessing Harmful Impacts of the DHS Shutdown on Americans, Part II"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Wednesday’s hearing came at the request of committee Democrats who had also requested White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller and border czar Tom Homan appear.</p><p>The two Trump administration officials did not appear. All but one of the committee’s 17 Republican members, committee chairman Andrew Garbarino, R-NY, did not attend Wednesday’s hearing either.</p><p>The Homeland Security Committee hearing was called to discuss the impacts of the Department of Homeland Security shutdown that has now lasted 67 days, the longest partial government shutdown in history. Senate Democrats have blocked DHS funding and are calling for reforms to ICE and CBP tactics.</p><p>When questioned by Rep. Al Green, D-Texas, Martinez said she thinks about the October shooting “all the time,” she has “sometimes” lost sleep over it and had nightmares shortly after it happened. At one point, she stood up, removed her jacket and showed the lawmakers where on her body she was shot.</p><p>Martinez noted that after being shot, she continued driving about a mile to a mechanic shop, where workers helped her and called 911.</p><p>“Imagine if I would’ve stayed, I wouldn’t be here to testify. I wouldn’t,” Martinez said.</p><p>Exum apparently bragged to other CBP agents about shooting Martinez. In one text message previously shown in court, he wrote, “I fired 5 rounds and she had 7 holes. Put that in your book boys.”</p><p>Martinez said Wednesday it’s “not fair” that she was charged in relation to the shooting while Exum has not.</p><p>“I’ve been through hell and back, and I’ve fought for my freedom, I’ve fought for my voice,” Martinez said. “And these agents, Charles Exum has not even been held accountable for his actions. But when it comes to me, I had to fight. What about him?”</p><p>Toward the end of the roughly one-and-a-half hour hearing, Martinez said she wants Exum to be charged and tried for the shooting.</p><p>“I want the world to see my pain, my trauma, because this is not something to joke about, right,” Martinez said. “This is my life, we’re talking about my life.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/03/dhs-kristi-noem-senate-judiciary-committee-testimony-marimar-martinez-dick-durbin" target="_blank" ></a></p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/20/operation-midway-blitz-marimar-martinez-anthony-ruiz-us-supreme-court-feds-drop-case-against-woman-shot-by-border-patrol-agent-in-brighton-park" target="_blank" ><u></u></a></p><p></p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/05/trump-fires-noem-dhs-ice-border-patrol-markwayne-mullin" target="_blank" ></a></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Fiscal del condado no denunció públicamente a Trump porque quería 'mantener' relaciones con el gobierno federal
entries.summary: <p>La principal fiscal del Condado de Cook, Eileen O'Neill Burke, se abstuvo de criticar a la administración de Trump en las semanas previas al Operativo Midway Blitz, ya que quería mantener sus "excelentes relaciones laborales" con las fuerzas del orden federal, según muestran los registros judiciales recién presentados.</p><p>Esto evidencia que O'Neill Burke tiene un conflicto que justifica la instalación de un fiscal especial que podría investigar a los agentes federales detrás de la campaña de deportación del otoño pasado, argumentaron una coalición de funcionarios electos y miembros de la comunidad.</p><p>"La fiscal del estado tiene una alianza con las fuerzas del orden federal que le impide tomar medidas contra los agentes federales que supuestamente han cometido delitos durante el Operativo Midway Blitz", escribieron sus abogados en un documento judicial de 30 páginas presentado el viernes.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-5d0000" name="module-5d0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>La lucha sobre si se debe nombrar un <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/13/coalicion-dice-que-fiscal-del-condado-de-cook-eileen-oneill-burke-ignoro-la-violencia-de-ice" target="_blank" >fiscal especial</a> podría llegar a un punto culminante pronto, ya que la jueza del circuito del Condado de Cook, Erica Reddick, está programada para escuchar argumentos el viernes. Ella es la juez presidente de la división criminal de la corte. La oficina de O'Neill Burke prometió una "respuesta integral" durante los argumentos.</p><p>La coalición que busca un fiscal especial señaló en su nueva presentación un correo electrónico del 11 de agosto. Enviado por un miembro del Consejo Asesor de Justicia, se discutía un esfuerzo por parte de la oficina de la presidenta de la Junta del Condado de Cook, Toni Preckwinkle, para lograr que las agencias del condado firmaran una declaración “respondiendo a las recientes acciones y retórica de la administración de Trump”.</p><p>El presidente Donald Trump <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/08/11/donald-trump-trump-federal-takeover-dc-police-national-guard-chicago-threat-jb-pritzker" target="_blank" >amenazó</a> ese mismo día con desplegar a la Guardia Nacional en Chicago. Trump eventualmente intentó cumplir con esa amenaza, pero fue bloqueado primero por <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/10/09/federal-judge-set-to-hear-arguments-in-high-stakes-national-guard-hearing" target="_blank" >la jueza federal April Perry</a> y eventualmente por la <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/donald-trump/2025/12/23/supreme-court-trump-national-guard-illinois" target="_blank" >Corte Suprema</a>. El entonces portavoz de O'Neill Burke, Matt McGrath, respondió el 12 de agosto, la mañana después de la amenaza de Trump.</p><p>"Obviamente compartimos preocupaciones sobre las acciones y retórica de Trump", escribió. "Al mismo tiempo, la prioridad principal de la fiscal del estado sigue siendo combatir las armas ilegales, y para continuar haciendo eso de manera efectiva necesitamos mantener nuestras excelentes relaciones laborales con la [Oficina de Alcohol, Tabaco, Armas de Fuego y Explosivos] local y otros socios federales. Así que no podremos firmar".</p><p>El Operativo Midway Blitz comenzó cuatro semanas después del correo electrónico, una copia del cual fue presentada el viernes con el último escrito de la coalición en la litigación sobre un fiscal especial.</p><p>"Si la fiscal del estado considera que su conflicto derivado de las colaboraciones federales le impide siquiera oponerse a las amenazas de la administración de Trump hacia Chicago, entonces seguramente el mismo conflicto le impide investigar y procesar a los agentes federales que llevaron a cabo esas amenazas poco después", escribieron los abogados de la coalición en su presentación del viernes.</p><p>La coalición que busca un fiscal especial está compuesta por más de 200 funcionarios electos, clérigos, periodistas y abogados. Incluye al Chicago News Guild, que representa a periodistas del Chicago Sun-Times. El esfuerzo es dirigido en la corte por el bufete de abogados Loevy and Loevy.</p><p>La oficina de O'Neill Burke insistió el martes en que está "horrorizada por la conducta violenta e inapropiada de los agentes del [Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas] en Chicago y en otros lugares".</p><p>Su personal también señaló que han "denunciado de manera reiterada y constante el abuso de la ley y la confianza pública por parte de la administración de Trump, incluyendo la presentación de un informe amistoso para evitar el despliegue de la Guardia Nacional en Chicago".</p><p>"La petición que busca un fiscal especial es frívola, contiene acusaciones sin fundamento y tergiversaciones graves de la ley, y dificultará que la oficina de la fiscal del estado del Condado de Cook cumpla con su labor de protección del público", dijo la oficina de O'Neill Burke en su comunicado.</p><p>Proseguir contra los agentes federales en la corte estatal requeriría demostrar que actuaron de manera irrazonable mientras cumplían con sus deberes. Mientras tanto, fiscales como O'Neill Burke tienen la discreción de elegir qué casos perseguir.</p><p>O'Neill Burke ha insistido en que cualquier investigación debe ser llevada a cabo por las fuerzas del orden. Su oficina presentó un informe amistoso instando a Perry a bloquear el despliegue de la Guardia Nacional. Y señaló un fallo de la Corte Suprema de Illinois de 2017 que dice que limita su propia capacidad para investigar a los agentes.</p><p>La alta corte encontró que el deber de un fiscal del estado de investigar es "limitado a circunstancias donde otras agencias de aplicación de la ley no manejan adecuadamente dicha investigación... o donde una agencia del orden público pide al fiscal del estado asistencia".</p><p>Pero la coalición argumentó el viernes que "es perfectamente claro que la policía local no está investigando". Señalaron la falta de cualquier pesquisa por parte de la policía de Franklin Park o de Chicago sobre el asesinato en septiembre de <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/09/16/silverio-villegas-gonzalez-quien-fue-asesinado-por-agentes-de-ice-toco-la-vida-de-tantos" target="_blank" >Silverio Villegas González</a> a manos de un agente federal.</p><p>La coalición argumentó que las circunstancias actuales son similares a las que llevaron a la designación de fiscales especiales en dos casos de alto perfil anteriores: la muerte de Laquan McDonald a manos del oficial de policía de Chicago Jason Van Dyke, y la de David Koschman a manos del sobrino del exalcalde Richard M. Daley, Richard J. “R.J.” Vanecko.</p><p>"Hay una base suficiente para inferir que la falta de acción de la fiscal del estado se debe a que está sujeta a las fuerzas del orden y que no está dispuesta a arriesgar la debilitación de relaciones políticas e institucionales”, escribieron los abogados de la coalición. “Así como en esos casos, aquí también debería ser nombrado un fiscal especial”.</p><p><i>Ninguno de los reporteros y editores que trabajaron en este reportaje —incluyendo a algunos representados por el Chicago News Guild— han estado involucrados en la demanda descrita en este artículo. </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Mune-ball: White Sox’ offense firing on all cylinders with Murakami at the wheel
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — Welcome to The Mune-ball Era for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a>.</p><p>They’re scrimping with an $82 million payroll, the third-lowest in Major League Baseball. They’re running out a slew of rookies and reclamation projects, some of them learning new positions to get their bats in the lineup. And they’re hitting the snot out of the ball, at least over the past week.</p><p>While general manager Chris Getz’s rebuilding squad might not be on a trajectory for a data-driven, Hollywood-written playoff run a la Billy Beane’s Moneyball-era Oakland Athletics, Getz’s first splashy free-agent splurge has supercharged an offense that’s finally making Sox games fun to watch again.</p><p>“We're very much connecting from top to bottom in the lineup, and I think it's just really important that we really continue as a team to get good results,” first-base phenom Munetaka Murakami said through an interpreter after hitting a home run <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/21/munetaka-murakami-homer-four-straight-games-white-sox-arizona" target="_blank" ><u>for the fourth straight game</u></a> of the Sox’ West Coast road trip.</p><p>The Japanese import’s two-year, $34 million contract has looked like a bigger bargain with every foot of his blasts entering play Wednesday. The last four of his nine dingers on the young season collectively traveled a third of a mile and tied him with Aaron Judge for second behind MLB home run leader Yordan Alvarez.</p><p>“I'm really analyzing the pitchers as much as possible so that I'm getting ready into the at-bats, so I'll just try to keep doing whatever I'm doing right now,” said Murakami, who regularly admires his towering shots out of the box but is quick to share credit with his teammates.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/18/colson-montgomery-white-sox-homers" >Colson Montgomery</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/31/white-sox-miguel-vargas-miami-grand-slam" >Miguel Vargas</a> and other cogs of the rebuild would do well to just keep doing what they’ve done against the A’s and Diamondbacks, who weathered 12 Sox homers over a four-game stretch. Murakami (.234/.394/.584), Vargas (.177/.320/.392) and Montgomery (.213/.323/.500) launched the Sox’ first back-to-back-to-back blasts in six years on Tuesday.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">on repeat 🔁 <a href="https://t.co/y1zELxyS5e">pic.twitter.com/y1zELxyS5e</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2046990514365296906?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 22, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>It has all helped the Sox jump from MLB’s worst team OPS entering their trip to 21st (.688), and shake off mid-April vibes that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/16/white-sox-head-west-with-that-familiar-losing-feeling-rays" target="_blank" ><u>scarcely could’ve been worse</u></a> after getting swept at home by the Rays. Their .986 OPS and 14 homers over a seven-game stretch were both tops in baseball.</p><p>Murakami’s the one getting the international spotlight, but “I think it’s just anyone,” Montgomery said. “Everything’s contagious, hits are contagious, good energy is contagious, and I think that’s what we’re doing right now.”</p><p>“It’s just settling in. A lot of us, we’re younger guys and we haven’t really played in April, kicked off the season, stuff like that,” Montgomery said. “Everyone is playing free, easy and just committed to their plan.”</p><p>After a hitting drought over the first couple weeks of the season, manager Will Venable says his batting order is enjoying a “good regression to that mean, where we’re able to get actual results.”</p><p>“You've gotta slug in this league. No matter when it is in the count, you've got to take your shot. I think we do have a number of guys [who] can leave the ballpark and slug. And so those are the guys that you've got to have take some shots,” Venable said.</p><p>“We have a number of guys that maybe that's not part of their game, and those are the guys that really need to be grinding at-bats, having that Plan-B swing, shooting the ball the other way and really extending the starting pitcher and working at-bats,” Venable said. “I think that's a good thing about our offense, that we have a bunch of different guys that can make an impact in different ways. We've just got to string it all together.”</p><p>The Sox’ power surge has left only two questions: how long can they keep it up? And when will they come up with a regular dugout home run celebration?</p><p>“We’re working on it right now. We have something in mind that’s coming,” Montgomery teased. “We don’t really want to tell anyone yet, but you’ll notice it.”</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: NFL draft: History shows stars are hard to find at No. 25 overall
entries.summary: <p>The odds say the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> won’t find a superstar with the 25<sup>th</sup> overall pick Thursday night.</p><p>History agrees.</p><p>No Pro Football Hall of Famer has ever been chosen No. 25 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/21/mock-draft-will-the-bears-take-blake-miller-kayden-mcdonald-or-t-j-parker-at-no-25" target="_blank" >in the draft</a>. Only 11 players picked No. 25 overall since the NFL-AFL merger have made the Pro Bowl. Of those, only five went more than twice — receiver Stanley Morgan (drafted in 1977), defensive lineman and onetime Bear Ted Washington (1991), linebacker Jon Beason (2007), cornerback Xavier Rhodes (2013) and center Tyler Linderbaum (2022). Linderbaum, whose rookie contract ended in March, became the highest-paid center in the history of the sport when the Raiders gave him a three-year, $81 million deal in March.</p><p>Linebacker Dont’a Hightower, class of 2012, won three Super Bowls with the Patriots. Receiver Santonio Holmes, who was named MVP of Super Bowl XLIII with the Steelers and spent the last season of his career with the Bears, was picked in 2006.</p><p>Safety Jabril Peppers and quarterback Tim Tebow were college superstars who didn’t turn into serviceable pros. Jaxson Dart will soon find out whether he belongs on that list, too — the Giants traded up with the Texans to draft the quarterback last year. He started 12 games and finished fourth in Offensive Rookie of the Year voting.</p><p>The Jaguars took running back Travis Etienne 25<sup>th</sup> in 2022. Since then, he’s run for 3,798 yards, the 10<sup>th</sup>-most in the NFL. Receiver Brandon Aiyuk was picked in the same spot a year before him — and finished seventh in the NFL in receiving yards in his third season. Holywood Brown, the 25<sup>th</sup> choice in 2019, had 1,008 receiving yards in his third year with the Ravens before bouncing to the Cardinals, Chiefs and now Eagles.</p><p>Thursday will mark the first time in the modern era of the draft that the Bears have picked 25th.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-bc0001" name="list-bc0001"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, items=[{type=Image, _styledTemplate=null, imageAlignment=null, isFeatured=false, description=[{items=[Edwards isn’t working on the field during the offseason training program, but is expected to be ready by the start of training camp in July.]}], wordCount=207, title=Bears LB T.J. entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: WNBA has record 216-game TV and streaming schedule across several outlets
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — A record 216 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/wnba" >WNBA</a> regular-season games and events will be available on multiple broadcast platforms throughout the year, the league announced Wednesday.</p><p>The league, in its 30th season, will have games on ABC/ESPN, CBS, Amazon Prime Video, ION, NBC, USA Sports and NBA TV.</p><p>"The WNBA is coming off a landmark season in 2025, one that was defined by incredible basketball and countless memorable performances," WNBA Commissioner Cathy Engelbert said. "With an incredible <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/13/sky-draft-gabriela-jaquez-with-no-5-pick" >2026 draft </a>and so many notable free agency signings and trades now giving way to the start of the season, the WNBA's unprecedented lineup of linear and streaming partners will shine a light on must-see matchups, and the remarkable skills the WNBA's stars provide each time they step on the court."</p><p>ESPN and ABC will broadcast 30 games in honor of the milestone year for the league. Thirteen of those, including the All-Star Game, will be on ABC. The network will have a doubleheader opening weekend with Caitlin Clark and Indiana facing Paige Bueckers and Dallas. The second game will feature a rematch of the WNBA Finals with champion Las Vegas hosting Phoenix.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">The SKY are scheduled for 29 national TV appearances. The breakdown:<br><br>ION - 8<br>CBS - 6<br>USA - 6<br>ESPN - 3<br>Prime - 3<br>ABC - 1<br>NBATV - 1<br>NBC - 1<br><br>Local broadcasts will air on The U (WCIU-Channel 26).</p>— Jeff Agrest #🟦 (@JeffAgrest) <a href="https://twitter.com/JeffAgrest/status/2047028174526071283?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 22, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>CBS will have eight prime-time games this year — up from two last season.</p><p>ION will continue to have its Friday night doubleheaders, broadcasting more than 50 games. Amazon will showcase 31 games this season on its service, including the WNBA Commissioner's Cup championship on June 30.</p><p>The WNBA returns to NBC with seven games on Sundays. The network will also show 12 games on Monday on Peacock. NBC will show the WNBA Finals this season with games either on that channel or on USA Network. Peacock will stream every game of the championship.</p><p>USA Network will also show 48 games on Wednesday and Monday nights. NBA TV will have a 15-game schedule.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" entries.title: Gold pocket watch of Titanic victim John Jacob Astor IV sells for $800K at auction in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>A gold pocket watch that John Jacob Astor IV was wearing on board the Titanic when the ship sank in 1912 sold at auction Wednesday in Chicago for $800,000. </p><p>Freeman’s, the West Loop auction house handling the sale, had predicted the timepiece would fetch between $300,000 and $500,000. The watch buyer is an Irish businessman, according to Freeman's.</p><p>A gold pencil case also recovered from Astor’s body went for $160,000, after fierce bidding that started at $22,500.</p><p>“These results show a rare convergence of history, craftsmanship, and human narrative,” said Reginald Brack, SVP, Head of Department, Watches at Freeman’s. “It is uncommon to find property that unites four significant names — John Jacob Astor IV, the RMS Titanic, Tiffany & Co., and Patek Philippe. Each has its own legacy, but together they tell a unique and moving story. The market’s response confirms these objects’ emotional and historical significance to collectors worldwide.”</p><p>Astor bought the watch from Tiffany & Co.’s flagship store in New York. It is engraved with his initials and keeps good time, according to Freeman’s.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/04/21/titanic-john-jacob-astor-iv-gold-pocket-watch-patek-philippe-auction" >Gold pocket watch of Titanic’s wealthiest passenger goes up for sale in Chicago</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/things-to-do/2026/04/15/titanic-a-voyage-through-time-chicago-vr-experience" >Immersive VR experience opening soon in Chicago lets you explore the Titanic</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Astor was arguably the most famous person on board the Titanic and certainly the wealthiest. He was a New York real estate baron traveling back to the United States from a months-long holiday in Europe with his pregnant wife, Madeleine Astor.</p><p>She boarded a lifeboat and survived; he had hoped to climb aboard the same lifeboat, but was told at the time that only women and children were allowed. His body was among the 330 plucked from the North Atlantic. He was found wearing a life jacket.</p><p>Little information was made available Wednesday about the winning bidders for either the pocket watch or the pencil case. The timepiece went to a bidder calling in by phone. The pencil case went to an online bidder, who had bid for but lost out on the watch.</p><p>Both items were offered as part of the estate of distant Astor relative Charlene Marshall, who died last year.</p>
entries.author: Stefano Esposito

entries.title: Review: Hayley Williams brings out American Football’s Mike Kinsella at Aragon in moment of emo superpower
entries.summary: <p>Hayley Williams’ fans got the memo when she bade them to “Love Me Different” at the Aragon Tuesday. As a break came in the song, the packed room started chanting her name and throwing up heart hands in a show of total adoration. The moment came off so heartfelt, it overwhelmed her for a moment.</p><p>“I guess the lesson in life is to take the f------ compliment,” she said, deciding to own it. “We are learning to love ourselves better in 2026!”</p><p>It’s this kind of affable authenticity that has made Williams your favorite artist’s favorite artist (Chappell Roan included) and why so many fans have stayed allegiant over the years, from her teenage start in Paramore that rose through the pop-punk/emo ranks in the mid-2000s to a maturing solo career displayed on Grammy-nominated 2025 album “Ego Death at a Bachelorette Party,” her first album on her Post-Atlantic Records indie label since breaking ties with the major label after 20 years. She speaks from the heart and isn’t afraid to break hers wide open and show all the pieces.</p><p>So it’s no wonder fans are coming out in droves to support the pop-punk queen on her first solo tour, which is sold out in every market.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a70000" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Hayley Williams performs Tuesday at Aragon Ballroom, the first of two sold-out shows in Chicago.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zachary Gray</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Excited fans came prepared, some handing out friendship bracelets in the bathroom (a holdover from Williams appearing on Taylor Swift’s Eras Tour), others wearing veils in homage to the album theme and some bearing sashes decorated with “F--- Morgan Wallen,” aka the “racist country singer” called out by Williams on the “Ego Death at a Bachelorette Party” title track. After he was caught on video hurling racial slurs in 2021, Williams has never let people forget it, continuing the beef at a recent gig in New York where she brought out Jason Isbell for a duet of the latter’s "Cover Me Up,” a song that Wallen loves to cover.</p><p>There was a special guest in Chicago, too: American Football’s Mike Kinsella who assisted on Williams’ recent hit “Parachute” in a moment of emo superpower. While that collab choice was less acerbic than New York, the spirit of calling-it-like-it-is was still alive and well at Aragon.</p><p>The night started with “Mirtazapine,” an ode to antidepressants delivered with such ’90s rock fervor it felt like a throwback to Prozac nation. Before the performance, a mock commercial came over the speakers, touting the drug’s supposed usage for anyone who finds our “phobic, fascist, racist, sexist” world “overwhelming,” and warning of side effects like “realizing this doesn’t have to be our reality.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e50000" name="image-e50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/38189c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+212/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F51%2F8f%2F01b05b934450b12aafbf6635bdaf%2Fhw-chicago-night-1-digital-02.jpeg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a27a2bc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+212/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F51%2F8f%2F01b05b934450b12aafbf6635bdaf%2Fhw-chicago-night-1-digital-02.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Hayley Williams performs Tuesday, April 22, 2026 at Aragon Ballroom." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a27a2bc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+212/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F51%2F8f%2F01b05b934450b12aafbf6635bdaf%2Fhw-chicago-night-1-digital-02.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a3f5998/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+212/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F51%2F8f%2F01b05b934450b12aafbf6635bdaf%2Fhw-chicago-night-1-digital-02.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a27a2bc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+212/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F51%2F8f%2F01b05b934450b12aafbf6635bdaf%2Fhw-chicago-night-1-digital-02.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Hayley Williams performs Tuesday at Aragon Ballroom. Across 90 minutes, Williams played every song off her new record, the Grammy-nominated “Ego Death at a Bachelorette Party.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zachary Gray</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b></b> Williams has long made a career singing about the “misery business,” but where Paramore songs are steeped in youthful ennui, Williams’ solo material elevates the talking points into important conversations of the moment. This air of seriousness parlayed into the visuals too — long known for vibrant pops of color in her hair and clothing, Williams instead paired muted blonde hair with a neutral slip skirt and baggy shirt as if an attempt to not distract and turn all the focus onto her songwriting. <b></b></p><p>One of the most powerful moments came with a piano-led performance of “True Believer” that packed in such poetic fury it should win a Pulitzer. “There are a lot of ghosts here and we are gonna dance with them tonight,” Williams shared, before pulling America’s skeletons out of the closet on the song. “The South will not rise again, ’til it's paid for every sin,” the Tennessee native declares in the lyrics, also referencing Billie Holiday’s harrowing “Strange Fruit.” It was followed with a cover of Nina Simone’s “Don’t Let Me Be Misunderstood” with a clear message to take Williams at her word because she will never go quiet about what matters.</p><p>Across 90 minutes, Williams continued to play every song off her new record at Aragon, much to the delight of the diehards in the crowd who belted out “Ice In My OJ” and “Hard” like they were personal mantras.</p><p>“It’s so healing and so good to feel connected to you,” Williams told them after, also berating the financial and mechanical roadblocks that often prevent fans from attending concerts and feeling moments like these. “It’s a crime how hard and inaccessible live music has become. … We all deserve this. It’s not a luxurious thing, it’s a human thing.”</p><p>If Williams is hinting at anything with her “bachelorette party,” it’s taking the next big step in her career — a vow to being more transparent, independent and fearless than ever.<br><b></b><br><i>Hayley Williams returns to Aragon for a second sold-out show Wednesday.</i></p><h3>Hayley Williams set list for April 22, 2026 show at Aragon Ballroom</h3><p>Mirtazapine<br>Showbiz<br>Disappearing Man<br>Zissou<br>Ice in My OJ<br>Hard<br>Kill Me<br>Blood Bros<br>Ego Death at a Bachelorette Party<br>Whim<br>Glum<br>Negative Self Talk<br>True Believer<br>Don’t Let Me Be Misunderstood (Nina Simone cover)<br>Brotherly Hate<br>Love Me Different<br>Dream Girl in Shibuya<br>Good Ol’ Days</p><p><b>Encore</b><br>Discovery Channel<br>I Won’t Quit on You<br>Parachute (with Mike Kinsella)</p>
entries.author: Selena Fragassi

entries.title: Family of man fatally shot by Chicago police in West Ridge files lawsuit
entries.summary: <p>The family of a 20-year-old man fatally shot by Chicago police last December in West Ridge filed a wrongful death lawsuit in federal court against the city and two police officers.</p><p>Roberto Calvario Jr.’s family alleges he “did not do anything threatening” when two officers approached his sedan late Dec. 9 in the 5700 block of North Washtenaw Avenue to investigate a report of “suspicious” activity and a potential stolen car.</p><p>Calvario suffered one gunshot to his head, and an officer was briefly dragged by the sedan before it crashed into a parked car, according to the lawsuit and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/police-reform/2026/01/22/chicago-police-fatal-shooting-west-ridge-roberto-calvario-jr" target="_blank" ><u>body-worn camera video</u></a> of the shooting.</p><p>The lawsuit was filed Wednesday, on what would have been Calvario’s 21st birthday, by attorneys from Loevy and Loevy, a civil rights law firm with a long history of suing over police misconduct.</p><p>Officer Jose Salazar fired the shot before being dragged by the sedan, the lawsuit alleges. It’s unclear from the chaotic bodycam videos when exactly the deadly shot was fired.</p><p>The officers encountered Calvario for about 10 seconds before was shot, according to the lawsuit and videos.</p><p>The officers are seen running up to Calvario’s car and yelling “stop,” as another person flees. The lawsuit claims the officer tried to “wrench” Calvario from the car and then shot him.</p><p>Calvario’s girlfriend was in the front passenger seat of the car. An officer grabbed her out of the car and placed her next to Calvario on the snowy street as officers checked on Calvario, the video shows.</p><p>Ten minutes elapsed before an ambulance arrived at the scene while Calvario lay breathing but unresponsive, the video shows. Calvario died hours later at a hospital.</p><p>Officers found a gun in Calvario’s waistband, and the Civilian Office of Police Accountability reported that a second weapon was recovered.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/McDk321huBY?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Man fatally shot in head by Chicago police 'posed no threat,' family's lawyers say"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Calvario’s girlfriend was arrested and held overnight at a police station, but no charges were filed, according to the lawsuit, which also alleges unlawful arrest.</p><p>The lawsuit also alleges the officers tried to “cover up” the shooting. They claimed through “inaccurate and misleading” police reports that Calvario attacked them with a weapon and that one officer was dragged by Calvario’s car, allegedly justifying the shooting as self-defense.</p><p>A spokesperson for the Chicago Law Department said Wednesday evening that the office had not been served with the lawsuit and it does not comment on pending litigation.</p><p>The lawsuit lists 14 counts of alleged civil rights violations, including wrongful death, excessive force, false arrest, infliction of emotional distress and conspiracy.</p><p>“Roberto — a young man who should have had his whole life ahead of him — is dead because this officer shot him, without cause, within seconds of encountering him,” Loevy and Loevy attorney Maggie Filler said in a statement.</p><p>“Our clients, [Calvario’s mother and his girlfriend], have suffered loss and trauma that no one should ever have to endure,” Filler said. “They are right to demand justice, and we are confident a jury will give it to them.”</p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: West Suburban Medical Center property owner files suit to take control of hospital
entries.summary: <p>The owner of the property West Suburban Medical Center sits on filed a lawsuit Wednesday to wrestle control of the hospital from his business partner. </p><p>New Jersey-based Ramco Healthcare Holdings filed the complaint in Cook County Circuit Court requesting a judge to appoint a receiver to take control of West Suburban's operations, allowing it to reopen. </p><p>The suit was filed against Dr. Manoj Prasad, CEO and majority owner of Resilience Healthcare, the private company that owns West Suburban Medical Center and Weiss Memorial Hospital. The minority owner of Resilience and sole owner of Ramco is Rathnaker Reddy Patlola. Ramco owns the hospital property.</p><p>In the lawsuit, Patlola accuses Prasad of "financial mismanagement and malfeasance." The complaint also seeks financial damages for back-rent Prasad allegedly owes Patlola. </p><p>“The appointment of a receiver is necessary to protect the public interest as the Oak Park community relies on the prompt reopening of (West Suburban) for essential healthcare services, particularly for its low-income population,” the suit reads. </p><p>Patlola owns all of Resilience’s hospital properties: the main Oak Park campus, the River Forest campus, Weiss and all associated medical office buildings. Last week, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/04/14/west-suburban-medical-center-river-forest-campus-eviction-manoj-prasad" target="_blank" >Prasad filed his own suit</a> against Patlola to stop him from attempting to evict Resilience from all three hospital properties. </p><p>Patlola also accuses Prasad of misusing a $10 million loan the state of Illinois gave to Resilience in May 2025 to fund operations at the now-closed Weiss, which had been the safety-net hospital in Uptown. Instead, the suit claims Prasad moved the funds to a separate bank account and "misappropriated" the funds for his "direct or indirect benefit." </p><p>The lawsuit also accuses Prasad of breaching the hosptials' leases and demands back rent totaling over $20 million. </p><p>“Without the immediate appointment of a receiver, there is substantial risk that Dr. Prasad will continue to misappropriate West Suburban’s remaining assets, further deteriorating the West Suburban Medical Center Property and eliminating any possibility of resuming hospital operations," according to the lawsuit. </p><p>In a statement, Prasad said the lawsuit is "without merit." </p><p>"The operative lease agreement between the parties, which Mr. Patlola signed, is already before the court in the complaint Resilience Healthcare filed last week. That agreement explicitly prohibits Mr. Patlola from terminating the lease or removing the hospital operator. Nothing in today's filing changes that," Prasad said. </p><p>He also said the claim about the mismanaged $10 million is false. </p><p>"Those funds were sent to an account co-owned by Mr. Patlola and were used for hospital payroll and operating expenses. Mr. Patlola knows this," Prasad said. </p><p>On March 25, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/03/25/west-suburban-medical-center-closing-runs-out-cash-weiss-memorial" target="_blank" >Prasad announced he was temporarily closing</a> West Suburban Medical Center and furloughing the vast majority of employees. He blamed the hospital’s year-old electronic medical record system “that has never functioned correctly” for the payroll issues. As a result, the hospital was operating on 10% to 15% of its usual revenue, he said.</p><p>Meanwhile, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/03/29/west-suburban-medical-center-owner-reopen-oak-park" target="_blank" >Patlola said he also was working</a> to reopen the hospital — but under new management. He said in a statement he was in talks with Insight Hospital & Medical Center to see if the company could take over West Suburban. In a news conference earlier this month, Prasad pushed back on the idea and said he was the man who could get the hospital back up and running.</p><p>Some <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/04/15/west-suburban-medical-center-reopening-some-clinics-amid-eviction-fight" target="_blank" >outpatient clinics resumed at West Suburban</a> last week. Prasad reiterated Wednesday that he hopes to have the hospital fully reopened by July.</p><p>Read Patlola's full complaint: </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://docs.google.com/viewer?embedded=true&url=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/ca/47/c635546a4860b39c1bf310fd28cc/ramco-complaint-against-prasad.pdf"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: Bears exit from Chicago closer to reality as House mulls bill helping Arlington Heights bid
entries.summary: <p>SPRINGFIELD — Illinois lawmakers inched closer to the red zone in keeping the Chicago Bears from crossing state borders into Indiana, with the Illinois House on Wednesday preparing to take up a measure that would give the NFL team property tax certainty while also providing some property tax relief for homeowners.</p><p>The megaproject proposal, which still needs approval from the Illinois Senate, would incentivize the Bears to build a domed stadium in Arlington Heights by negotiating payments in lieu of property taxes, known as PILOT, based on assessments. It also directs those funds toward property tax relief for homeowners — both near the megaproject and throughout Illinois.</p><p>The bill represents the first major legislative step toward ensuring the Bears stay in Illinois. The thorny details of infrastructure funding for Arlington Heights — which are likely to include help for Chicago — must still be dealt with before lawmakers adjourn from the spring session next month.</p><p>State Rep. Kam Buckner, D-Chicago, the lead sponsor on the measure, this week added a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/04/20/bears-stadium-proposed-legislation-arlington-heights-property-tax-relief-illinois-homeowners-northwest-indiana-site" >property tax relief sweetener </a>to try to secure some bipartisan support and to squash the optics of billionaire owners getting a special deal. His latest proposal was approved by the House Revenue Committee by a 15-5 vote Wednesday afternoon.</p><p>The latest proposal would put 50% of special PILOT payments into a property tax relief fund that would then be split between 60% toward property tax rebates for homeowners in the district where the megaproject is located, and 40% into the Illinois Property Tax Relief Fund. Buckner, before a House panel Wednesday, called the provision the piece he “is most proud of.”</p><p>“This is not a tax holiday,” Buckner said. “The project still pays property taxes on a frozen basis, and it must also make a negotiated special payment.”</p><p>The project must invest at least $100 million in eligible costs to qualify — and the megaproject developer must enter into an incentive agreement with the local municipality, a project labor agreement with a goal of putting 20% or more of project-related contracts to minority-owned businesses and having the company’s CEO consider the economic benefits the project brings to underserved communities.</p><p>A local review board would also be created with members of the local taxing bodies, like school districts and others, to decide the amount of the special payment from the developers on a weighted vote.</p><p>Buckner said language was added to exclude data centers from the legislation after lawmakers expressed concerns.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-240000" name="image-240000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/096f7f0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Fd5%2F8309cc0240aba9931ceb2bcc3a24%2Fimg-9792.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/faf0702/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Fd5%2F8309cc0240aba9931ceb2bcc3a24%2Fimg-9792.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4798059/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Fd5%2F8309cc0240aba9931ceb2bcc3a24%2Fimg-9792.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="State Rep. Kam Buckner, D-Chicago, briefed lawmakers on the latest changes to the megaprojects bill during a House Revenue Committee hearing on Wednesday afternoon. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4798059/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Fd5%2F8309cc0240aba9931ceb2bcc3a24%2Fimg-9792.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c6bc53/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Fd5%2F8309cc0240aba9931ceb2bcc3a24%2Fimg-9792.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4798059/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5712x3206+0+539/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F44%2Fd5%2F8309cc0240aba9931ceb2bcc3a24%2Fimg-9792.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>State Rep. Kam Buckner, D-Chicago, briefed lawmakers on the latest changes to the megaprojects bill during a House Revenue Committee hearing on Wednesday afternoon. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tina Sfondeles/Chicago Sun-Times </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Other changes include what Buckner called “accountability measures” requiring the developer and municipality to give a report to the Illinois General Assembly every five years. The latest language changed a sunset from five to seven years.</p><p>Buckner has emphasized that while the Bears have received all the attention on the legislation, the proposal is intended to help the state go head to head with states like Michigan, Ohio and Texas, which have enacted megaproject legislation of their own.</p><p>“This is about making Illinois competitive, but in a way that keeps people in the center and focus of this,” Buckner said. “Just because someone builds a bridge and someone is the first person to cross that bridge, doesn’t mean that bridge is built for them.”</p><p>The proposal would also bar state and local elected officials from receiving discounted tickets to stadium events connected to a megaproject — and bars all state officials, local officials and their representatives from working for any megaproject certificate holder if they personally and substantially participated in negotiation of the megaproject agreement.</p><p>Two Republican lawmakers — Rep. Martin McLaughlin, R-Barrington Hills, and Rep. Brad Stephens, R-Rosemont — voted to advance the measure. Other Republicans voted no, saying it would inadvertently raise taxes for property owners in other taxing districts that overlap with Arlington Heights but don’t actually include the site of the stadium project.</p><p>Buckner said he expects the measure to get the 60 Democratic votes needed to pass the House and advance to the Senate, which will return Tuesday after taking a week off.</p><p>In a statement, Illinois Senate President Don Harmon, D-Oak Park, offered little on what he thinks of the latest proposal, saying there are “ongoing conversations, and the Senate would certainly review any legislation the House sends over.”</p><p>Local school districts and school groups have long raised concerns on property tax valuations around the stadium site. Illinois Federation of Teachers President Stacy Davis Gates, in a memo obtained by WBEZ, said she is pleased with a change in the proposal that ensures school districts won’t get less money than what they would get in the first year of the project. But Gates, who also heads up the Chicago Teachers Union, said the plan doesn’t do enough to fund local public schools.</p><p> </p><p>“We don’t believe it sufficiently captures the new property value of these developments," Gates said, "so schools don’t get as much new revenue as we believe they’ll need.”</p>

Allsun O'Malley and Devin Archer" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aac8676/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+1158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fde%2Ffa%2F27486ce4495cb0e5997757c06d3f%2Fsouth-pacific-ids-allsun-omalley-and-devin-archer-photo-credit-jennifer-heim.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ecc833f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+1158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fde%2Ffa%2F27486ce4495cb0e5997757c06d3f%2Fsouth-pacific-ids-allsun-omalley-and-devin-archer-photo-credit-jennifer-heim.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aac8676/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+1158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fde%2Ffa%2F27486ce4495cb0e5997757c06d3f%2Fsouth-pacific-ids-allsun-omalley-and-devin-archer-photo-credit-jennifer-heim.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Allsun O’Malley and Devin Archer star in “South Pacific.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jennifer Heim</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-ccd606e0-3c1e-11f1-97f4-43fbcb901192"><li>Rodgers and Hammerstein’s musical <b>“South Pacific”</b> is a 10-time Tony Award-winner filled with classic songs. Set during World War II, it follows two love stories set against the beauty of a tropical island. The Paramount Theatre staging boasts 36 actor-singer-dancers and a live 15-piece orchestra. From April 29-June 14 at Paramount Theatre, 23 E. Galena Blvd., Aurora. Tickets: $31-$106. <a class="Link" href="http://paramountaurora.com" target="_blank" ><u>paramountaurora.com</u></a>.</li><li><b></b> Porchlight in Concert presents Stephen Sondheim and James Goldman’s <b>“Follies.” </b>The musical stars Michelle Duffy, Angela Ingersoll, Anthony Rapp and Stephen Wallem; Michael Weber directs. At 2 and 7:30 p.m. April 25 and 2 p.m. April 26 at Studebaker Theater, Fine Arts Building, 410 S. Michigan Ave. Tickets: $104.50-$159.50. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://porchlightmusictheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>porchlightmusictheatre.org</u></a>.</li></ul><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ec0000" name="image-ec0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/03ba3ce/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+563/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F18%2F35%2F67f29ceb4eb6af72abc1b5ccd246%2Floki-the-end-of-the-world-tour-actor-jack-chylinski-photo-credit-josh-bernaski.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/efd1980/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+563/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F18%2F35%2F67f29ceb4eb6af72abc1b5ccd246%2Floki-the-end-of-the-world-tour-actor-jack-chylinski-photo-credit-josh-bernaski.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cf87f78/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+563/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F18%2F35%2F67f29ceb4eb6af72abc1b5ccd246%2Floki-the-end-of-the-world-tour-actor-jack-chylinski-photo-credit-josh-bernaski.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="LOKI - The End of the World Tour - actor Jack Chylinski " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cf87f78/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+563/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F18%2F35%2F67f29ceb4eb6af72abc1b5ccd246%2Floki-the-end-of-the-world-tour-actor-jack-chylinski-photo-credit-josh-bernaski.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3ca7e91/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+563/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F18%2F35%2F67f29ceb4eb6af72abc1b5ccd246%2Floki-the-end-of-the-world-tour-actor-jack-chylinski-photo-credit-josh-bernaski.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cf87f78/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+563/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F18%2F35%2F67f29ceb4eb6af72abc1b5ccd246%2Floki-the-end-of-the-world-tour-actor-jack-chylinski-photo-credit-josh-bernaski.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jack Chylinski stars in the titular role in “Loki – The End of the World Tour” running from April 24-June 14 at Lifeline Theatre.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Josh Bernaski</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-ccd62df0-3c1e-11f1-97f4-43fbcb901192"><li><b>“Loki – The End of the World Tour”</b> is Christina Calvit’s world premiere rock musical that follows Loki (Jack Chylinski) as he arrives at Asgard, bringing chaos, comedy and three monstrous children as he tangles with Odin, Thor and Freya. Heather Currie directs. From April 24-June 14 at Lifeline Theatre, 6912 N. Glenwood Ave. Tickets: $45. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://lifelinetheatre.com" target="_blank" ><u>lifelinetheatre.com</u></a>.</li><li><b>“Barbra & Judy Together Again” </b>features Barbra Streisand impressionist Steven Brinberg and Judy Garland interpreter Angela Ingersoll. The performance is a benefit for Season of Concern Chicago, the organization that provides financial assistance for theater artists affected by illness, injury or circumstance. At 7 p.m. April 27 at Studebaker Theater, Fine Arts Building, 410 S. Michigan Ave. Tickets: $50-$200. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://fineartsbuilding.com/events" target="_blank" ><u>fineartsbuilding.com/events</u></a>.</li><li>Saint Sebastian Players present<b> “I Hate Hamlet,”</b> Paul Rudnick’s comedy about an actor cast as Hamlet who is visited by the ghost of legendary actor John Barrymore. Zach Kunde directs. From April 24-May 17 at St. Bonaventure, 1625 W. Diversey Pkwy. Tickets: $35. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://saintsebastianplayers.org" target="_blank" ><u>saintsebastianplayers.org</u></a>.</li><li><b>“The Wedding Singer”</b> is a Tony Award-nominated musical about a 1980s wedding singer who is the life of the party until his fiancée flees. Amber Mak directs. From April 29-May 24 at Metropolis Performing Arts Centre, 111 W. Campbell St., Arlington Heights. Tickets: $25-$49. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://metropolisarts.com" target="_blank" ><u>metropolisarts.com</u></a>. <b></b></li></ul><h3><b>Dance</b></h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-ccd62df1-3c1e-11f1-97f4-43fbcb901192"><li>The always remarkable <b>Alvin Ailey American Dance Theater</b> returns with performances of Medhi Walerski’s “Blink of an Eye,” Judith Jamison’s “A Case of You,” Fredrick Earl Mosley’s “Embrace,” Maija Garcia’s “Jazz Island,” Matthew Neenan’s “Difference Between,” Jamar Roberts’ “Song of the Anchorite” and each performance concludes with Ailey’s masterpiece “Revelations.” At 7:30 p.m. April 24, 1 p.m. and 7:30 p.m. April 25 and 3 p.m. April 26 at The Auditorium, 50 E. Ida B. Wells Dr. Tickets: $46+. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://auditoriumtheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>auditoriumtheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>Marcel Gbeffa and Sarah Trouche’s <b>“Didę” </b>is inspired by the Guèlèdè traditions whose ceremonies and masks pay homage to Ìyá Nlá, the Yoruba primordial spirit of all creation. At 7:30 p.m. April 24 at Dance Center of Columbia College, 1306 S. Michigan Ave. Tickets: $10-$100. At 7 p.m. April 23 at Music Box Theatre, 3733 N. Southport Ave. Tickets: $14. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://musicboxtheatre.com" target="_blank" ><u>musicboxtheatre.com</u></a>.</li><li><b>CineYouth Festival</b> presents films from filmmakers ages 22 and younger. Fullerton Ave. Admission is free, but reservations are required. Included are circus demonstrations and performers; interactive sound, set and costume design stations; art activities; a DJ and dancing; face painting; a peek into the orchestra pit and more. Enter at 11 a.m. or noon April 26 and stay until 2 p.m. at Lyric Opera, 20 N. Wacker. Tickets: $10, children $5. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://lyricopera.org" target="_blank" ><u>lyricopera.org</u></a>.</li><li><b></b> Georgette Kelly’s new play, <b>“Farewell Opportunity,”</b> is about a young girl who visits the NASA Jet Propulsion Lab and meets the Opportunity Mars Rover and the NASA scientist in charge. For ages 5 and up. Raquel Torre directs. From April 25-May 17 at Filament Theatre, 4041 N. Milwaukee Ave. Tickets: $7-$21. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://filamenttheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>filamenttheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>Chicago Public Library’s 27th annual <b>Poetry Fest</b> features poetry workshops and performances suitable for all ages. Among the highlights are a Haiku Fest, Poems While You Wait, a conversation with Illinois Poet Laureate Mark Turcotte and more. From 10 a.m.-4 p.m. April 25 at Harold Washington Library Center, 400 S. State St. Admission is free. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://chi.gov/poetryfest2026" target="_blank" ><u>chi.gov/poetryfest2026</u></a>. <b></b></li><li><b>Chicagoland Bookstore Crawl,</b> a one-day event celebrating indie bookstores, returns on April 25, Independent Bookstore Day. More than 80 city and suburban bookstores are participating. Get a map from your first store and see how many you can visit in a day. Reach 10 or 15 stores and receive 10% or 15% discounts from all participating stores for a year. For more information and a map of store locations, visit <a class="Link" href="http://chilovebooks.com" target="_blank" ><u>chilovebooks.com</u></a>.</li></ul>
entries.author: Mary Houlihan

entries.title: Doctor caught up in Loretto Hospital embezzlement scandal returns to Chicago from Dubai to face charges
entries.summary: <p>A doctor who owned multiple<b> </b>medical<b> </b>supply<b> </b>companies<b> </b>has returned to Chicago from overseas to face criminal charges<b> </b>for his role in a fraud scheme at Loretto Hospital<b> </b>that also led to charges against former hospital executives.</p><p>Sameer<b> </b>Suhail appeared in federal custody Wednesday<b> </b>in a 17th-floor courtroom at the Dirksen Federal Courthouse. U.S. Magistrate Judge Beth<b> </b>Jantz<b> </b>told him he could expect to be released later in the afternoon on a $5 million bond and into home confinement.</p><p>Federal authorities agreed to the arrangement,<b> </b>Assistant U.S. Attorney Kelly<b> </b>Guzman<b> </b>told the judge. The prosecutor said Suhail was taken into custody around 4 p.m. Tuesday.</p><p>Adam<b> </b>Sheppard, Suhail’s defense attorney, told reporters that Suhail returned to the United States voluntarily, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/08/02/loretto-hospital-anosh-ahmed-sameer-suhail-medicare-fraud-covid" target="_blank" >traveling from Dubai</a> into O’Hare Airport. During the hearing before Jantz, Sheppard entered a not guilty plea on Suhail’s behalf.</p><p>Suhail is charged in a 45-count<b> </b>indictment<b> </b>along with former Loretto executives George Miller, Anosh<b> </b>Ahmed, and<b> </b>Heather<b> </b>Bergdahl. It alleges that Ahmed, Suhail and Bergdahl schemed to put their hands on more than $15 million in hospital funds by submitting bogus documents claiming Loretto owed vendors for goods and services the hospital actually had not received.</p><p>Suhail also is accused of a scheme involving Ahmed and Miller to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2024/07/12/loretto-hospital-cfo-15-million-embezzlement" target="_blank" >steer hospital business<b> </b>to Suhail’s companies</a>.</p><p>In all, Suhail faces six counts of wire fraud, six counts of aiding and abetting embezzlement, a kickback conspiracy and two counts of money laundering, Guzman told Jantz.</p><p>Ahmed, the hospital’s onetime chief financial officer who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/06/17/former-loretto-hospital-executive-charged-fake-covid-testing-scheme" target="_blank" >resigned in 2021 amid a series of COVID-19 controversies</a>, was arrested in Serbia on Nov. 30, 2025. He has yet to return to Chicago to face charges.</p><p>Bergdahl, the hospital’s former chief transformation officer, is expected to plead guilty, court records show. Miller, the hospital’s former<b> </b>chief executive,<b> </b>is set to plead guilty April 30.</p><p>The troubles at Loretto date back to 2021, when Block Club Chicago reported that the hospital vaccinated employees at Trump Tower, where Ahmed had a condo.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/08/02/loretto-hospital-anosh-ahmed-sameer-suhail-medicare-fraud-covid" target="_blank" >Ex-West Side hospital exec, friend accused in massive Medicare scam are living large in Dubai</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2024/07/12/loretto-hospital-cfo-15-million-embezzlement" target="_blank" >Loretto Hospital’s embattled ex-CFO charged in $15M embezzlement scheme</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/06/17/former-loretto-hospital-executive-charged-fake-covid-testing-scheme" target="_blank" >Former Loretto Hospital executive accused in $293 million fake COVID-testing scheme</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Bears GM Ryan Poles must solve persistent problems on offensive, defensive lines in NFL Draft
entries.summary: <p>It’s a surprising thing for a team run by a former offensive lineman, but fortifying the lines of scrimmage has been the most persistent problem during Ryan Poles’ time as the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a>’ general manager.</p><p>The offensive and defensive lines are far from exciting things to discuss when it comes to roster-building, but they’re essential. Along with great quarterback play, those units are at the core of any winning team.</p><p>Poles has tried everything to fix the lines, yet they remain unfinished as he goes into his fifth draft Thursday. It’s another chance for him to get it right, and left tackle, defensive end and defensive tackle must be the Bears’ top priorities in the first and second rounds with the Nos. 25, 57 and 60 picks.</p><p>If the Bears stay at No. 25, the best offensive tackles in that range likely will be Alabama’s Kadyn Proctor, Georgia’s Monroe Freeling and Clemson’s Blake Miller. Picking one of them would spare coach Ben Johnson and quarterback Caleb Williams from another prolonged battle at left tackle.</p><p>At defensive end, the projections put Miami’s Akheem Mesidor, Missouri’s Zion Young, Auburn’s Keldric Faulk, Texas A&M’s Cashius Howell and Clemson’s T.J. Parker as possibilities. The options at defensive tackle probably will be Clemson’s Peter Woods and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/25/as-ohio-state-star-kayden-mcdonald-prepares-to-visit-bears-he-says-im-their-no-1-guy" target="_blank" >Ohio State’s Kayden McDonald</a>.</p><p>The options improve if the Bears trade up, of course, but Poles generally hasn’t wanted to mortgage future draft capital.</p><p>Plenty has gone wrong for the Bears on both lines in the last five years — some their fault and some beyond their control.</p><p>Poles thought the offensive line was set after an overhaul last year, which included using a second-round pick to draft left tackle Ozzy Trapilo. Then <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/30/bears-coach-ben-johnson-uncertainty-at-left-tackle-over-next-several-years-after-ozzy-trapilo-injury" target="_blank" >Trapilo suffered a career-jeopardizing knee injury</a> in the playoffs and Pro Bowl <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/03/bears-drew-dalman-expected-to-retire-center-ryan-poles-falcons-stanford-nfl" target="_blank" >center Drew Dalman stunningly retired</a> at 27.</p><p>The Bears have said Trapilo will miss most or all of this season. And with his status uncertain beyond that, they would be prudent to make a long-term move by drafting a left tackle at No. 25. If Trapilo comes back strong in 2027 and there’s a surplus at the position, the Bears will have a great trade asset.</p><p>It’s a different story on the defensive line.</p><p>Poles didn’t think he had any choice but to unload future Hall of Fame defensive end Khalil Mack when he took the job and would defend rescinding the splashy offer he made to free-agent defensive tackle Larry Ogunjobi after a failed physical.</p><p>Mack has continued to be a force and Ogunjobi started for the Steelers for the next three seasons, but there was reasonable logic for those decisions at the time.</p><p>There should be no grace, however, for some of the others, and that’s where Poles must make corrections in the draft.</p><p>Defensive end Montez Sweat has been good, but the Bears needed him to be great after trading a second-round pick to get him at the trade deadline in 2023 and signing him to a four-year, $98 million contract extension that makes him their highest-paid player. His 21½ sacks since the trade rank 18th in the NFL.</p><p>Then there was the combined $90.8 million the Bears threw at defensive tackle Grady Jarrett (turning 33 next week) and defensive end Dayo Odeyingbo (16½ career sacks before signing) last offseason. Neither made the impact the Bears hoped, and Odeyingbo suffered a torn Achilles at midseason.</p><p>The Bears are locked into both deals until an affordable out arises next offseason, so the draft is their only option.</p><p>Poles also drafted defensive tackles Gervon Dexter and Zacch Pickens 53rd and 64th in 2023, a significant investment. But the Bears cut Pickens last year, and Dexter still has a ways to go to reach the potential they saw in him. Shemar Turner, a defensive end they selected in the second round last year, is an unknown after suffering a torn ACL after playing only five games.</p><p>It’s almost impossible to be a good defense without a disruptive pass rush and imposing run-stuffers. The Bears somewhat offset those deficiencies last season by leading the league with 33 takeaways, but Johnson admitted that’s not a reliable equation.</p><p>The best teams are great at the boring stuff, elements people usually only notice when they’re missing. The Bears need to protect Williams and be steady in the running game. They need to speed up opposing quarterbacks in the pocket and stop letting running backs shred them. All of that starts up front, which makes their picks Thursday and Friday crucial to their future.</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.title: Garfield Park Conservatory set to open revamped Children’s Garden
entries.summary: <p>Kids can once again play among the exotic plants of the Garfield Park Conservatory as the 118-year-old institution reopens its renovated Elizabeth Morse Genius Children’s Garden to the public on May 1, with tickets available starting Friday.</p><p>“The new EMG Garden reflects our commitment to creating spaces where every child can feel seen, inspired and connected to the natural world,” said Chicago Park District General Superintendent and CEO Carlos Ramirez-Rosa in a press release. </p><p>After 20 years of wear and tear on the original play area, the Conservatory decided the space needed a makeover. Now, five years later, children can enjoy a variety of interactive zones, including a toddler area, an art and nature gallery, and a towering structure called the High-Biscus Climber. The 20-foot net climbing structure provides a panoramic view of the Conservatory’s gardens and glasshouses.</p><p>Jennifer Van Valkenburg is President and CEO of Garfield Park Conservatory. </p><p>“We are thrilled to be able to provide this improved resource to the community,” she said in a statement. “We wanted to ensure it would be available for even more generations to experience a connection to nature year-round through immersive play and exploration.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-560000" name="image-560000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Children climb up the High-Biscus Climber, an interactive 20-foot net structure that’s part of the renovated Children’s Garden at the Garfield Park Conservatory.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The Park District said the new Garden, much of which was funded by private donations, is “designed for every child.”</p><p>It includes a play area for children with sensory disabilities to enjoy a quiet space while taking a break from the playtime chaos as well as a wheelchair-friendly spiral ramp. The Conservatory also said that kits will be available for visitors who want sound reducing headphones or other sensory-friendly tools.</p><p>The play area is located at the Garfield Park Conservatory, 300 N. Central Park Ave., Chicago and will be open Wednesdays from 10 a.m. to 8 p.m. and Thursdays through Sundays from 10 a.m. to 5 p.m.</p><p>Reservations with timed ticket entry are required for all parents and children. Tickets are free for Chicagoans, members and children younger than 7. Non-Chicago residents must pay $10 for adults or $5 for seniors age 65 and up, as well as $5 for children ages 7 to 17.</p>
